abstract: Camera and human motion controls have been extensively studied for video generation, but existing approaches typically address them separately, suffering from limited data with high-quality annotations for both aspects. To overcome this, we present Uni3C, a unified 3D-enhanced framework for precise control of both camera and human motion in video generation. Uni3C includes two key contributions. First, we propose a plug-and-play control module trained with a frozen video generative backbone, PCDController, which utilizes unprojected point clouds from monocular depth to achieve accurate camera control. By leveraging the strong 3D priors of point clouds and the powerful capacities of video foundational models, PCDController shows impressive generalization, performing well regardless of whether the inference backbone is frozen or fine-tuned. This flexibility enables different modules of Uni3C to be trained in specific domains, i.e., either camera control or human motion control, reducing the dependency on jointly annotated data. Second, we propose a jointly aligned 3D world guidance for the inference phase that seamlessly integrates both scenic point clouds and SMPL-X characters to unify the control signals for camera and human motion, respectively. Extensive experiments confirm that PCDController enjoys strong robustness in driving camera motion for fine-tuned backbones of video generation. Uni3C substantially outperforms competitors in both camera controllability and human motion quality. Additionally, we collect tailored validation sets featuring challenging camera movements and human actions to validate the effectiveness of our method.

keyInsights: A modular, lightweight control module can effectively imbue large, frozen video diffusion models with precise camera control without sacrificing their generalization capabilities.
Explicitly aligning all 3D control signals (camera and human) into a unified 3D world space is crucial for generating physically plausible and visually consistent videos.
Strong 3D priors, particularly point clouds, are more effective for precise camera trajectory control and faster convergence than Plücker rays alone, especially when combined.
originalProblem: Existing video generation models lack precise, unified control over both camera trajectories and human motions.
Many control methods heavily modify foundational video diffusion models, impairing generalization and requiring costly, extensive joint training data.
Disparate 3D control signals (e.g., scenic geometry and human poses) often lead to incoherent guidance and physically unrealistic video outputs.
results: PCDController achieves superior camera control precision, demonstrating significantly lower Absolute Translation Error (ATE), Relative Translation Error (RPE), and Relative Rotation Error (RRE) compared to existing methods.
Uni3C substantially outperforms competitors in unified camera-human motion control, yielding higher visual quality and consistency as validated by VBench++ scores and user studies.

The framework enables flexible human motion transfer from diverse sources, supports detailed hand control via Hamer models, and maintains robust performance even with imperfect depth-derived point clouds.
solution: A lightweight, plug-and-play camera control module (PCDController) is introduced, trained with a frozen foundational VDM backbone using 3D geometric priors like warped point clouds and Plücker ray embeddings.
A Global 3D World Guidance mechanism aligns diverse 3D conditions—scenic point clouds, SMPL-X human models, and Hamer hand models—into a consistent 3D space during inference.
The framework integrates with advanced human animation backbones (e.g., RealisDance-DiT) by externally adding PCDController features, enabling seamless, unified control without joint fine-tuning.
paper_summary.summary: The Uni3C framework from Alibaba DAMO Academy and Fudan University unifies precise 3D-enhanced camera and human motion controls for video generation, utilizing a lightweight, plug-and-play module and global 3D world guidance to achieve superior control accuracy and video quality compared to existing methods. This approach reduces hallucination rates while supporting flexible motion transfer and preserving foundational model generalization.

title: Uni3C: Unifying Precisely 3D-Enhanced Camera and Human Motion Controls for Video Generation

abstract:
Motion control is crucial for generating expressive and compelling video
content; however, most existing video generation models rely mainly on text
prompts for control, which struggle to capture the nuances of dynamic actions
and temporal compositions. To this end, we train a video generation model
conditioned on spatio-temporally sparse or dense motion trajectories. In
contrast to prior motion conditioning work, this flexible representation can
encode any number of trajectories, object-specific or global scene motion, and
temporally sparse motion; due to its flexibility we refer to this conditioning
as motion prompts. While users may directly specify sparse trajectories, we
also show how to translate high-level user requests into detailed, semi-dense
motion prompts, a process we term motion prompt expansion. We demonstrate the
versatility of our approach through various applications, including camera and
object motion control, "interacting" with an image, motion transfer, and image
editing. Our results showcase emergent behaviors, such as realistic physics,
suggesting the potential of motion prompts for probing video models and
interacting with future generative world models. Finally, we evaluate
quantitatively, conduct a human study, and demonstrate strong performance.
Video results are available on our webpage: this https URL

keyInsights: Spatio-temporal point trajectories provide a unified and robust representation for diverse motion control tasks, including object manipulation, camera control, and motion transfer.
Training on dense tracks enables strong generalization, allowing the model to perform effectively with varying numbers of tracks, localized tracks, and temporally sparse constraints during inference.
The system reveals emergent behaviors, such as realistic physics, indicating the underlying video model's learned "world prior" and offering a tool to probe its understanding of dynamics.
originalProblem: Text prompts are inherently inadequate for conveying intricate nuances of motion in video generation, struggling with specifics like acceleration, exact trajectories, and timing.
Existing motion conditioning techniques often lack flexibility, struggle with error accumulation (e.g., optical flow), or require specialized adapters for different motion types.
Generating precise, user-defined dynamic content in videos remained challenging due to the difficulty of translating high-level intent into granular motion control.
results: "Motion Prompting" consistently outperformed baselines (Image Conductor, DragAnything) in quantitative appearance metrics (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, FVD) and achieved significantly lower End-Point Error for denser tracks on the DAVIS dataset.
A human study showed strong preference for the proposed method, with win rates ranging from 73.7% to 80.5% across motion adherence, motion quality, and visual quality criteria.

The framework enables a wide range of applications, including interactive image manipulation (e.g., animating hair), fine-grained object control (e.g., rotating a cat's head), realistic camera movements, and motion transfer between videos.
solution: Introduced spatio-temporally sparse or dense point trajectories (motion prompts) as a flexible and expressive conditioning signal, encoding 2D coordinates and visibility flags for any number of points.

Integrated this motion representation into the pre-trained Lumiere video diffusion model via a ControlNet adapter, trained in a single stage using dense tracks extracted by BootsTAP from a large video dataset.
Developed "motion prompt expansion" techniques to translate high-level user inputs (e.g., mouse drags, geometric primitives, depth-based camera paths) into detailed motion trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Google DeepMind and partner institutions introduced "Motion Prompting," a framework for controlling video generation using spatio-temporal point trajectories. This method, built on the Lumiere model, enables fine-grained control over object and camera movements, achieving superior motion adherence and visual quality compared to baselines in both quantitative metrics and human evaluations.

title: Motion Prompting: Controlling Video Generation with Motion Trajectories

abstract:
Customized text-to-video generation aims to produce high-quality videos that
incorporate user-specified subject identities or motion patterns. However,
existing methods mainly focus on personalizing a single concept, either subject
identity or motion pattern, limiting their effectiveness for multiple subjects
with the desired motion patterns. To tackle this challenge, we propose a
unified framework VideoMage for video customization over both multiple subjects
and their interactive motions. VideoMage employs subject and motion LoRAs to
capture personalized content from user-provided images and videos, along with
an appearance-agnostic motion learning approach to disentangle motion patterns
from visual appearance. Furthermore, we develop a spatial-temporal composition
scheme to guide interactions among subjects within the desired motion patterns.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that VideoMage outperforms existing methods,
generating coherent, user-controlled videos with consistent subject identities
and interactions.

keyInsights: Motion patterns can be learned independently of visual appearance through careful disentanglement
Gradient-based fusion of multiple subject LoRAs enables coherent multi-subject generation
Spatial attention regularization helps maintain distinct spatial arrangements of subjects
originalProblem: Existing text-to-video methods can only customize either subject identity or motion patterns, but not both simultaneously
Current approaches suffer from "appearance leakage" where reference motion videos inadvertently affect generated subject appearances
results: Outperforms existing methods like DreamVideo and MotionDirector in preserving subject identities and motion fidelity
Successfully prevents appearance leakage while achieving desired subject interactions
Human preference study confirms superior performance across text alignment, subject fidelity, motion fidelity, and video quality
solution: Separate Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules for capturing subject identity and motion patterns
Appearance-agnostic motion learning using negative classifier-free guidance
Spatial-temporal collaborative composition scheme for guiding multi-subject interactions
paper_summary.summary: A framework from National Taiwan University and NVIDIA enables simultaneous customization of multiple subject identities and their interactive motions in text-to-video generation through appearance-agnostic motion learning and spatial-temporal collaborative composition, addressing the appearance leakage problem while maintaining high video quality and temporal coherence.

title: VideoMage: Multi-Subject and Motion Customization of Text-to-Video  Diffusion Models

abstract: Existing video generation models excel at producing photo-realistic videos from text or images, but often lack physical plausibility and 3D controllability. To overcome these limitations, we introduce PhysCtrl, a novel framework for physics-grounded image-to-video generation with physical parameters and force control. At its core is a generative physics network that learns the distribution of physical dynamics across four materials (elastic, sand, plasticine, and rigid) via a diffusion model conditioned on physics parameters and applied forces. We represent physical dynamics as 3D point trajectories and train on a large-scale synthetic dataset of 550K animations generated by physics simulators. We enhance the diffusion model with a novel spatiotemporal attention block that emulates particle interactions and incorporates physics-based constraints during training to enforce physical plausibility. Experiments show that PhysCtrl generates realistic, physics-grounded motion trajectories which, when used to drive image-to-video models, yield high-fidelity, controllable videos that outperform existing methods in both visual quality and physical plausibility. Project Page: this https URL

keyInsights: Embedding physics priors (via an MPM-derived physics loss) directly into a diffusion model allows for robust learning of complex, physically plausible dynamics across diverse material types.
The proposed spatio-temporal attention architecture in the denoising network effectively models both local particle interactions and global temporal consistency in 3D point trajectories.

Utilizing 3D point trajectories as an intermediate representation offers a flexible and efficient control signal for guiding pre-trained video generation models, enabling fine-grained control over object motion.
originalProblem: Current video generative models lack physical plausibility and explicit 3D controllability over dynamic behaviors, often producing unnatural motions.
Traditional physics simulators are computationally expensive, sensitive to hyperparameters, prone to numerical instabilities, and challenging to integrate efficiently into generative pipelines.
Existing controllable video generation methods typically operate in 2D or offer coarse control, failing to provide inherent 3D physical understanding or consistent object deformations.
results: PhysCtrl achieved the highest average scores in GPT-4o evaluations for image-to-video generation, scoring 4.5 in semantic adherence, 4.5 in physical commonsense, and 4.3 in video quality, surpassing state-of-the-art baselines.
The generative dynamics model demonstrated superior performance in trajectory generation, with a vIoU of 77.59%, Chamfer Distance of 0.0028, and L2-distance of 0.0015, outperforming M2V and MDM.

Ablation studies confirmed that spatial attention, temporal attention, and the physics loss are crucial components, each contributing significantly to the model's ability to generate accurate and physically plausible dynamics.
solution: A two-stage framework that lifts an input image to a 3D point cloud, predicts physics-grounded 3D point trajectories using a generative physics network, and then synthesizes a video from these trajectories.
A diffusion-based generative physics network learns the conditional distribution of 3D point trajectories, incorporating user-specified material properties and external forces.
The network employs novel spatio-temporal attention blocks and is trained on a large synthetic dataset of 550K object animations, regularized by diffusion, velocity, physics, and boundary losses.
paper_summary.summary: The PhysCtrl framework introduces a method for generating physically plausible videos from a single image, enabling explicit control over material properties and external forces. It leverages a diffusion-based generative physics network to produce 3D point trajectories, outperforming existing video generation and dynamics models in physical adherence, video quality, and trajectory accuracy.

title: PhysCtrl: Generative Physics for Controllable and Physics-Grounded Video Generation

abstract: Recent advances in video generation have led to remarkable improvements in visual quality and temporal coherence. Upon this, trajectory-controllable video generation has emerged to enable precise object motion control through explicitly defined spatial paths. However, existing methods struggle with complex object movements and multi-object motion control, resulting in imprecise trajectory adherence, poor object consistency, and compromised visual quality. Furthermore, these methods only support trajectory control in a single format, limiting their applicability in diverse scenarios. Additionally, there is no publicly available dataset or benchmark specifically tailored for trajectory-controllable video generation, hindering robust training and systematic evaluation. To address these challenges, we introduce MagicMotion, a novel image-to-video generation framework that enables trajectory control through three levels of conditions from dense to sparse: masks, bounding boxes, and sparse boxes. Given an input image and trajectories, MagicMotion seamlessly animates objects along defined trajectories while maintaining object consistency and visual quality. Furthermore, we present MagicData, a large-scale trajectory-controlled video dataset, along with an automated pipeline for annotation and filtering. We also introduce MagicBench, a comprehensive benchmark that assesses both video quality and trajectory control accuracy across different numbers of objects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MagicMotion outperforms previous methods across various metrics. Our project page are publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A progressive training procedure, moving from dense to sparse trajectory conditions while always providing a first-frame mask, effectively transfers knowledge and significantly improves control accuracy for sparser inputs.

The Latent Segmentation Loss (LSL) can guide fine-grained object shape consistency in the latent space, reducing deformation issues and improving control fidelity even when only sparse (bounding box) guidance is provided.

Curating a large-scale, high-quality, domain-specific dataset like MagicData, with rigorous filtering for motion and annotation, is crucial for developing robust and controllable video generation models.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art video generation models lack precise, fine-grained control over specific object or camera movements.
Existing trajectory control methods are often limited to a single control signal (e.g., points, masks), which can be either labor-intensive for dense inputs or imprecise for sparse ones.
There is a scarcity of large-scale, publicly available datasets and comprehensive benchmarks specifically designed for trajectory-controllable video generation.
results: MagicMotion significantly outperformed seven leading trajectory-controllable I2V methods on the MagicBench and DAVIS datasets, achieving an FID of 15.06, FVD of 112.69, Mask IoU of 91.57%, and Box IoU of 87.75% on MagicBench.
The model demonstrated robust multi-object control, maintaining superior performance across categories ranging from 1 to more than 5 controlled objects, a challenging aspect for existing methods.

Ablation studies confirmed that MagicData, the progressive dense-to-sparse training, and the Latent Segmentation Loss are critical components, each contributing substantially to improved video quality, temporal consistency, and trajectory adherence.
solution: Developed MagicMotion, a Diffusion Transformer-based framework integrating a Trajectory ControlNet that supports a dense-to-sparse spectrum of trajectory conditions, including segmentation masks, bounding boxes, and sparse bounding boxes.

Implemented a progressive training strategy from dense masks to sparse bounding boxes, coupled with a Latent Segmentation Loss (LSL), to enhance object consistency and shape perception under sparse guidance.

Created MagicData, the first large-scale public dataset (23K videos) for trajectory-controllable video generation, and MagicBench, a comprehensive benchmark (600 videos) featuring multi-object control evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: MagicMotion, developed by Fudan University and Microsoft Research Asia, proposes a framework for controllable video generation that guides object movements from a single image using dense-to-sparse trajectories. The system, along with its introduced MagicData dataset and MagicBench evaluation platform, achieved an FID of 15.06, FVD of 112.69, and Mask IoU of 91.57% on MagicBench, outperforming existing trajectory-controllable methods.

title: MagicMotion: Controllable Video Generation with Dense-to-Sparse Trajectory Guidance

abstract:
The development of Text-to-Video (T2V) generation has made motion transfer
possible, enabling the control of video motion based on existing footage.
However, current methods have two limitations: 1) struggle to handle
multi-subjects videos, failing to transfer specific subject motion; 2) struggle
to preserve the diversity and accuracy of motion as transferring to subjects
with varying shapes. To overcome these, we introduce \textbf{ConMo}, a
zero-shot framework that disentangle and recompose the motions of subjects and
camera movements. ConMo isolates individual subject and background motion cues
from complex trajectories in source videos using only subject masks, and
reassembles them for target video generation. This approach enables more
accurate motion control across diverse subjects and improves performance in
multi-subject scenarios. Additionally, we propose soft guidance in the
recomposition stage which controls the retention of original motion to adjust
shape constraints, aiding subject shape adaptation and semantic transformation.
Unlike previous methods, ConMo unlocks a wide range of applications, including
subject size and position editing, subject removal, semantic modifications, and
camera motion simulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ConMo
significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in motion fidelity and
semantic consistency. The code is available at
this https URL

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Separating compound motions into individual subject and background components enables better control
Using Local Spatial Marginal Means (LSMM) helps isolate motion cues for overlapping subjects
Blending subject motion with camera trajectory control weights reduces semantic constraints
originalProblem: Existing motion transfer methods struggle with multiple subjects and complex motion patterns
Current approaches have difficulty preserving motion fidelity when transferring to subjects with different shapes and semantics
results: Achieves better text alignment and motion fidelity compared to baseline methods
Successfully handles multiple subject scenarios and shape adaptations
Enables new applications like subject size/position editing and camera motion simulation
Shows some limitations with incomplete mask segmentation
solution: Motion disentanglement stage separates individual subject motions using subject masks and local feature differences
Soft guidance strategy enables flexible motion recomposition during video generation while maintaining motion consistency
Integration with Video Diffusion Models for spatiotemporal coherence
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University and BUPT introduce ConMo, a framework for zero-shot motion transfer in text-to-video generation that disentangles and recomposes individual subject motions from reference videos, enabling precise control over multiple subjects while preserving motion accuracy across different shapes and semantics.

title: ConMo: Controllable Motion Disentanglement and Recomposition for  Zero-Shot Motion Transfer

abstract:
Camera control, which achieves diverse visual effects by changing camera
position and pose, has attracted widespread attention. However, existing
methods face challenges such as complex interaction and limited control
capabilities. To address these issues, we present OmniCam, a unified multimodal
camera control framework. Leveraging large language models and video diffusion
models, OmniCam generates spatio-temporally consistent videos. It supports
various combinations of input modalities: the user can provide text or video
with expected trajectory as camera path guidance, and image or video as content
reference, enabling precise control over camera motion. To facilitate the
training of OmniCam, we introduce the OmniTr dataset, which contains a large
collection of high-quality long-sequence trajectories, videos, and
corresponding descriptions. Experimental results demonstrate that our model
achieves state-of-the-art performance in high-quality camera-controlled video
generation across various metrics.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Decomposing camera control into discrete motion representations enables a unified pipeline for diverse input modalities.
Leveraging 3D reconstruction and diffusion models allows for generating consistent video content from novel camera perspectives.
End-to-end optimization, specifically using reinforcement learning, refines both trajectory generation and video synthesis for improved output quality.
originalProblem: Existing video generation methods lack intuitive and precise control over camera movements, limiting creative potential.
Current approaches struggle to maintain spatial structure and content coherence during complex camera motions.
There is a lack of comprehensive datasets for training and evaluating camera-controlled video generation models.
results: OmniCam achieves better perceptual similarity (lower LPIPS) and structural similarity (higher SSIM) compared to state-of-the-art methods like ViewCrafter and FSGS.
User studies indicate superior performance across multiple dimensions, particularly in guidance following and video motion quality.
The introduced OmniTr dataset contains over 10,000 camera trajectory sequences with corresponding text descriptions, facilitating training and evaluation for camera-controlled video generation.
solution: OmniCam employs a two-stage framework: first generating a precise camera trajectory from various inputs (text, video, or direct specification), then synthesizing video guided by this trajectory.
It utilizes a discrete motion representation as an intermediate step to convert user inputs (text parsed by an LLM, video trajectories extracted via SLAM) into a continuous camera path.

Video synthesis combines 3D scene reconstruction using DUSt3R, initial rendering along the trajectory, and diffusion-based refinement with end-to-end reinforcement learning optimization to ensure visual quality and spatial consistency.
paper_summary.summary: OmniCam introduces a unified framework for camera-controlled video generation that supports diverse input modalities like text and reference videos. The system generates videos with precise, frame-level camera movements while maintaining spatial consistency and achieving improved perceptual and structural similarity compared to prior methods.

title: OmniCam: Unified Multimodal Video Generation via Camera Control

abstract: Camera and object motions are central to a video's narrative. However, precisely editing these captured motions remains a significant challenge, especially under complex object movements. Current motion-controlled image-to-video (I2V) approaches often lack full-scene context for consistent video editing, while video-to-video (V2V) methods provide viewpoint changes or basic object translation, but offer limited control over fine-grained object motion. We present a track-conditioned V2V framework that enables joint editing of camera and object motion. We achieve this by conditioning a video generation model on a source video and paired 3D point tracks representing source and target motions. These 3D tracks establish sparse correspondences that transfer rich context from the source video to new motions while preserving spatiotemporal coherence. Crucially, compared to 2D tracks, 3D tracks provide explicit depth cues, allowing the model to resolve depth order and handle occlusions for precise motion editing. Trained in two stages on synthetic and real data, our model supports diverse motion edits, including joint camera/object manipulation, motion transfer, and non-rigid deformation, unlocking new creative potential in video editing.

keyInsights: The use of 3D point tracks, coupled with an adaptive cross-attention mechanism for sampling and splatting, enables robust and 3D-aware control over both camera and object motion, crucial for handling occlusions and depth order.

Leveraging the full spatiotemporal context of the source video within a V2V diffusion framework is essential for maintaining visual coherence and accurately synthesizing the causal effects of edited motions.

A two-stage training pipeline, combining synthetic data for foundational learning and real-world data for domain adaptation, effectively overcomes the challenge of data scarcity for complex video motion editing tasks.
originalProblem: Existing video editing methods provide fragmented control over motion, often limited to either camera adjustments or simple object manipulations, lacking a unified framework for precise joint control.
Current motion-controlled Image-to-Video (I2V) models discard crucial spatiotemporal context from the input video, leading to visual inconsistencies and unrealistic outputs in edited scenes.
Accurately handling 3D complexities such as depth, occlusions, and synthesizing unseen regions during motion edits remains a significant challenge for many 2D-based or simplified 3D techniques.

Training sophisticated Video-to-Video (V2V) diffusion models for motion manipulation faces a severe data scarcity problem, as large datasets of paired videos with precise motion annotations are difficult to acquire.
results: Edit-by-Track quantitatively outperforms state-of-the-art I2V and V2V camera-controlled baselines on the DyCheck iPhone dataset and in-the-wild videos, demonstrating superior visual quality (lower LPIPS) and significantly better track control (lowest End-Point Error - EPE).

Qualitative results show the framework's ability to maintain input scene context and accurately synthesize causal effects of edited object motions (e.g., correct shadows, water splashes), avoiding inconsistencies seen in other methods.

The framework supports diverse editing applications including joint camera and object motion manipulation, human motion transfer, non-rigid shape deformation, object removal, duplication, and robustly handles partial track inputs.
solution: A track-conditioned Video-to-Video (V2V) diffusion model, named "Edit-by-Track," is developed by fine-tuning a pretrained Text-to-Video (T2V) diffusion model (Wan-2.1) to accept 3D point track conditions.

A specialized 3D track conditioner integrates adaptive cross-attention for sampling visual context from source video tokens based on projected 3D tracks, injecting depth cues, and adaptively splatting these context-rich tokens back into both source and target video spaces.

A two-stage training strategy addresses data scarcity: initial bootstrapping on synthetic Blender data establishes fundamental motion control, followed by fine-tuning on real-world monocular video pairs from an internal stock dataset to enhance generalizability.
paper_summary.summary: Edit-by-Track, developed by researchers from Adobe Research and the University of Maryland College Park, introduces a generative video motion editing framework that enables precise, joint manipulation of both camera and object movements using 3D point tracks. This approach maintains spatiotemporal context and synthesizes causal effects of motion edits, outperforming previous methods with superior visual quality and more accurate track control.

title: Generative Video Motion Editing with 3D Point Tracks

abstract: Recent advances in illumination control extend image-based methods to video, yet still facing a trade-off between lighting fidelity and temporal consistency. Moving beyond relighting, a key step toward generative modeling of real-world scenes is the joint control of camera trajectory and illumination, since visual dynamics are inherently shaped by both geometry and lighting. To this end, we present Light-X, a video generation framework that enables controllable rendering from monocular videos with both viewpoint and illumination control. 1) We propose a disentangled design that decouples geometry and lighting signals: geometry and motion are captured via dynamic point clouds projected along user-defined camera trajectories, while illumination cues are provided by a relit frame consistently projected into the same geometry. These explicit, fine-grained cues enable effective disentanglement and guide high-quality illumination. 2) To address the lack of paired multi-view and multi-illumination videos, we introduce Light-Syn, a degradation-based pipeline with inverse-mapping that synthesizes training pairs from in-the-wild monocular footage. This strategy yields a dataset covering static, dynamic, and AI-generated scenes, ensuring robust training. Extensive experiments show that Light-X outperforms baseline methods in joint camera-illumination control and surpasses prior video relighting methods under both text- and background-conditioned settings.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Explicitly decoupling geometric/motion cues from illumination cues and providing them as distinct conditioning signals to a diffusion model allows for robust and flexible joint manipulation of camera and lighting.

Combining fine-grained, per-frame geometric and lighting information with a global illumination token within a Diffusion Transformer architecture is crucial for maintaining both local visual fidelity and temporal consistency of lighting across generated video frames.

Synthetic training data generated via an 'inverse mapping' degradation strategy effectively addresses the scarcity of real-world multi-view and multi-illumination datasets, enabling the training of complex generative models for high-dimensional control tasks.
originalProblem: Prior research in video generation independently addressed either video relighting (lacking camera control) or camera-controlled synthesis (lacking illumination control), leaving their joint manipulation as an open challenge.
Real-world videos capture complex scenes with intertwined geometry, motion, and illumination, making disentangled and coherent control difficult from limited monocular input.

Training models for joint camera-illumination control requires extensive paired multi-view and multi-illumination video datasets, which are exceptionally challenging to acquire in real-world settings.
results: Light-X achieved an FID score of 101.06 for joint camera-illumination control, outperforming baselines (e.g., 122.73 for the best baseline combination), demonstrating superior relighting fidelity and motion preservation.

For video relighting tasks, Light-X recorded an FID of 83.65 and an Aesthetic score of 0.645 for text-conditioned relighting, showing both high lighting accuracy and temporal coherence compared to other methods.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of all three diverse training data sources (static, dynamic, AI-generated), the fine-grained and global lighting control components, and the degradation-based data curation strategy to the model's overall performance and robustness.
solution: A disentangled conditioning scheme that explicitly separates camera control (using dynamic point clouds for geometry/motion) and illumination control (using a single relit frame projected onto scene geometry).

A Diffusion Transformer-based video diffusion model (Light-DiT) that integrates fine-grained vision tokens (from projected source views and relit projections) and global illumination tokens (from the initial relit frame) for consistent control.

Light-Syn, a degradation-based data curation pipeline that synthesizes diverse paired multi-view and multi-illumination training data by inversely mapping ground truth geometry and illumination onto degraded in-the-wild videos.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from S-Lab at NTU, BAAI, and other institutions developed LIGHT-X, a framework for generative 4D video rendering that provides joint control over camera trajectory and scene illumination from a single monocular video. The model achieves superior perceptual quality and temporal consistency in generated videos, outperforming existing methods in both joint control and video relighting tasks.

title: Light-X: Generative 4D Video Rendering with Camera and Illumination Control

abstract: Existing methods for human motion control in video generation typically rely on either 2D poses or explicit 3D parametric models (e.g., SMPL) as control signals. However, 2D poses rigidly bind motion to the driving viewpoint, precluding novel-view synthesis. Explicit 3D models, though structurally informative, suffer from inherent inaccuracies (e.g., depth ambiguity and inaccurate dynamics) which, when used as a strong constraint, override the powerful intrinsic 3D awareness of large-scale video generators. In this work, we revisit motion control from a 3D-aware perspective, advocating for an implicit, view-agnostic motion representation that naturally aligns with the generator's spatial priors rather than depending on externally reconstructed constraints. We introduce 3DiMo, which jointly trains a motion encoder with a pretrained video generator to distill driving frames into compact, view-agnostic motion tokens, injected semantically via cross-attention. To foster 3D awareness, we train with view-rich supervision (i.e., single-view, multi-view, and moving-camera videos), forcing motion consistency across diverse viewpoints. Additionally, we use auxiliary geometric supervision that leverages SMPL only for early initialization and is annealed to zero, enabling the model to transition from external 3D guidance to learning genuine 3D spatial motion understanding from the data and the generator's priors. Experiments confirm that 3DiMo faithfully reproduces driving motions with flexible, text-driven camera control, significantly surpassing existing methods in both motion fidelity and visual quality.

keyInsights: Implicit, view-agnostic motion representations, learned by intentionally discarding spatial layout, can better leverage and elicit the intrinsic 3D priors of large-scale generative models than explicit 3D reconstructions.

Flexible, text-guided camera control naturally emerges as a byproduct of genuine 3D awareness, which is effectively fostered through view-rich training data and semantic (cross-attention) conditioning.
A progressive training curriculum, combined with annealing geometric guidance, provides a robust strategy for learning complex 3D-aware motion dynamics from diverse 2D video inputs.
originalProblem: Existing 2D pose-based methods rigidly bind motion to the driving perspective, preventing novel-view synthesis and dynamic camera movements for human video generation.

Methods relying on explicit 3D parametric models (e.g., SMPL) for conditioning suffer from inherent inaccuracies like depth ambiguity and limited expressiveness, which can override the generative model's native 3D priors.
A lack of robust mechanisms to decouple human motion control from camera control, limiting flexible camera manipulation and creative freedom in generated videos.
results: 3DiMo achieved state-of-the-art quantitative performance, demonstrating the lowest LPIPS (0.2206), FID (36.92), and FVD (297.4) scores compared to leading 2D pose-based and 3D SMPL-based methods, indicating superior perceptual quality and realism.
User studies confirmed 3DiMo's superiority, with the highest Mean Opinion Scores (MOS) in motion accuracy, naturalness (4.18), 3D physical plausibility (4.05), and overall visual quality.

The framework successfully generates accurate, high-fidelity, and physically plausible human videos, effectively reproducing driving motions under diverse text-guided camera configurations while resolving common depth ambiguities.
solution: A DiT-based video generation framework that integrates an implicit motion encoder, distilling compact, 1D view-agnostic motion tokens from 2D driving frames by discarding spatial layout and applying random perspective transformations.

Training on a comprehensive view-rich dataset, including single-view, multi-view, and moving-camera videos, combined with a progressive multi-stage learning strategy to foster genuine 3D motion understanding.
Utilizing auxiliary geometric supervision from parametric models, linearly annealed during early training, to accelerate spatial understanding and stabilize the learning of 3D-aware representations.
paper_summary.summary: The Kling Team at Kuaishou Technology, with collaborators from Tsinghua University and CASIA, introduced 3DiMo, a framework for 3D-aware human video generation that extracts implicit, view-agnostic motion from 2D driving videos. This approach achieves flexible text-guided camera control and generates high-fidelity, physically plausible human animations, demonstrating superior perceptual quality (LPIPS 0.2206) and realism (FID 36.92) compared to prior methods.

title: 3D-Aware Implicit Motion Control for View-Adaptive Human Video Generation

abstract:
Motion-controllable image animation is a fundamental task with a wide range
of potential applications. Recent works have made progress in controlling
camera or object motion via various motion representations, while they still
struggle to support collaborative camera and object motion control with
adaptive control granularity. To this end, we introduce 3D-aware motion
representation and propose an image animation framework, called
Perception-as-Control, to achieve fine-grained collaborative motion control.
Specifically, we construct 3D-aware motion representation from a reference
image, manipulate it based on interpreted user instructions, and perceive it
from different viewpoints. In this way, camera and object motions are
transformed into intuitive and consistent visual changes. Then, our framework
leverages the perception results as motion control signals, enabling it to
support various motion-related video synthesis tasks in a unified and flexible
way. Experiments demonstrate the superiority of the proposed approach. For more
details and qualitative results, please refer to our anonymous project webpage:
this https URL
title: Perception-as-Control: Fine-grained Controllable Image Animation with  3D-aware Motion Representation

abstract: Emerging video diffusion models achieve high visual fidelity but fundamentally couple scene dynamics with camera motion, limiting their ability to provide precise spatial and temporal control. We introduce a 4D-controllable video diffusion framework that explicitly decouples scene dynamics from camera pose, enabling fine-grained manipulation of both scene dynamics and camera viewpoint. Our framework takes continuous world-time sequences and camera trajectories as conditioning inputs, injecting them into the video diffusion model through a 4D positional encoding in the attention layer and adaptive normalizations for feature modulation. To train this model, we curate a unique dataset in which temporal and camera variations are independently parameterized; this dataset will be made public. Experiments show that our model achieves robust real-world 4D control across diverse timing patterns and camera trajectories, while preserving high generation quality and outperforming prior work in controllability. See our website for video results: this https URL

keyInsights: Injecting continuous temporal information directly into the attention mechanism (Time-RoPE) and modulating features (Time-AdaLN) allows for fine-grained world-time control within video diffusion models.
A unified 4D conditioning approach, combining spatio-temporal positional encodings and adaptive normalizations, effectively disentangles camera pose and world time.
Training on a purpose-built synthetic dataset that independently varies camera and temporal dynamics is crucial for learning robust, decoupled 4D control that generalizes to real-world scenarios.
originalProblem: Existing high-fidelity video diffusion models conflate "world time" (scene dynamics) with "camera pose" (viewpoint), limiting independent control.
Prior methods for controlling video generation often provide coarse temporal adjustments or rely on two-stage pipelines, leading to inconsistent 4D content.
The lack of disentangled spatio-temporal control hinders advanced applications like true 'bullet time' effects or interactive virtual experiences.
results: BulletTime achieved a pixel-level accuracy of 24.57 PSNR, 0.6905 SSIM, and 0.1265 LPIPS on synthetic data, outperforming adapted camera-controlled baselines by a notable margin.

On real-world videos, the model demonstrated highly precise camera control with the lowest rotation (1.47 RotErr) and translation (1.32 TransErr) errors, while also yielding superior temporal stability and consistency (lower FVD of 2292).

The framework successfully generated complex 'bullet time' effects and other scenarios requiring independent camera movement and continuous time manipulation, showing strong generalization capabilities beyond its human-centric synthetic training data.
solution: The framework fine-tunes a Diffusion Transformer (DiT), integrating continuous world-time control via Time-aware Positional Encoding (Time-RoPE) and Time-conditioned Adaptive Layer Normalization (Time-AdaLN).
It extends this control to 4D by incorporating camera viewpoint conditioning through a unified 4D Positional Encoding (4D-RoPE) and Camera-conditioned Adaptive Normalization (Camera-AdaLN).
A custom synthetic dataset was curated using PointOdyssey, designed to explicitly decouple temporal and camera variations, enabling the model to learn disentangled 4D control.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ETH Zurich, Stanford University, and CUHK developed BulletTime, a 4D-controllable video diffusion framework that explicitly decouples continuous world time from camera pose. The framework achieves superior pixel-level accuracy with 24.57 PSNR on synthetic data and demonstrates robust, accurate camera control in real-world settings with 1.47 rotation error, enabling novel cinematic effects like dynamic 'bullet time' sequences.

title: BulletTime: Decoupled Control of Time and Camera Pose for Video Generation

abstract: We propose MotionAgent, enabling fine-grained motion control for text-guided image-to-video generation. The key technique is the motion field agent that converts motion information in text prompts into explicit motion fields, providing flexible and precise motion guidance. Specifically, the agent extracts the object movement and camera motion described in the text and converts them into object trajectories and camera extrinsics, respectively. An analytical optical flow composition module integrates these motion representations in 3D space and projects them into a unified optical flow. An optical flow adapter takes the flow to control the base image-to-video diffusion model for generating fine-grained controlled videos. The significant improvement in the Video-Text Camera Motion metrics on VBench indicates that our method achieves precise control over camera motion. We construct a subset of VBench to evaluate the alignment of motion information in the text and the generated video, outperforming other advanced models on motion generation accuracy.
title: MotionAgent: Fine-grained Controllable Video Generation via Motion Field Agent

abstract: We present SpaceTimePilot, a video diffusion model that disentangles space and time for controllable generative rendering. Given a monocular video, SpaceTimePilot can independently alter the camera viewpoint and the motion sequence within the generative process, re-rendering the scene for continuous and arbitrary exploration across space and time. To achieve this, we introduce an effective animation time-embedding mechanism in the diffusion process, allowing explicit control of the output video's motion sequence with respect to that of the source video. As no datasets provide paired videos of the same dynamic scene with continuous temporal variations, we propose a simple yet effective temporal-warping training scheme that repurposes existing multi-view datasets to mimic temporal differences. This strategy effectively supervises the model to learn temporal control and achieve robust space-time disentanglement. To further enhance the precision of dual control, we introduce two additional components: an improved camera-conditioning mechanism that allows altering the camera from the first frame, and CamxTime, the first synthetic space-and-time full-coverage rendering dataset that provides fully free space-time video trajectories within a scene. Joint training on the temporal-warping scheme and the CamxTime dataset yields more precise temporal control. We evaluate SpaceTimePilot on both real-world and synthetic data, demonstrating clear space-time disentanglement and strong results compared to prior work. Project page: this https URL Code: this https URL

keyInsights: Explicitly disentangling animation time and source-aware camera control signals is crucial for robust, independent space-time manipulation, avoiding the limitations of standard frame indices.
Data augmentation via temporal warping on existing multi-view datasets is effective for teaching non-monotonic temporal control to video diffusion models.
A synthetic dataset with dense spatiotemporal coverage is critical for learning fine-grained, continuous 4D generative control and achieving robust disentanglement.
originalProblem: Existing generative video models lack joint, continuous, and disentangled control over both camera viewpoint (space) and scene motion (time).
Dynamic scene novel view synthesis methods are often computationally expensive, prone to artifacts, or limited by strictly monotonic temporal progression.
A scarcity of public datasets exists for training models to understand and generate dynamic scenes with continuous temporal and spatial variations.
results: Successfully achieves disentangled space and time control, allowing synthesis of novel views with independently altered motion sequences (e.g., slow motion, reverse, bullet time) while maintaining scene fidelity.
Demonstrates superior temporal control, achieving substantially higher PSNR and SSIM, and lower LPIPS across various temporal control categories on the Cam×Time test split compared to baselines.

Significantly improves camera control accuracy, exhibiting superior RelRot, RelTrans, AbsRot, and AbsTrans metrics on real-world datasets, and supporting arbitrary starting camera poses for generation.
solution: A latent video diffusion model is developed, incorporating novel conditioning for animation time and source-aware camera poses for explicit disentanglement.
A temporal warping augmentation scheme is applied during training to repurpose existing multi-view datasets and expose the model to diverse non-monotonic temporal behaviors.
A new synthetic 'Cam×Time' dataset is created in Blender, systematically sampling a dense grid of camera viewpoints and temporal states to provide exhaustive supervision for 4D control.
paper_summary.summary: SpaceTimePilot, developed by researchers from the University of Cambridge and Adobe Research, introduces a video diffusion model capable of independently controlling camera viewpoint and scene motion in dynamic videos. The system re-renders a single monocular video to synthesize novel views with effects like slow motion or reverse playback, outperforming baselines in both temporal and camera control metrics.

title: SpaceTimePilot: Generative Rendering of Dynamic Scenes Across Space and Time

abstract: Controllable video generation aims to synthesize video content that aligns precisely with user-provided conditions, such as text descriptions and initial images. However, a significant challenge persists in this domain: existing models often struggle to maintain strong semantic consistency, frequently generating videos that deviate from the nuanced details specified in the prompts. To address this issue, we propose SSG-DiT (Spatial Signal Guided Diffusion Transformer), a novel and efficient framework for high-fidelity controllable video generation. Our approach introduces a decoupled two-stage process. The first stage, Spatial Signal Prompting, generates a spatially aware visual prompt by leveraging the rich internal representations of a pre-trained multi-modal model. This prompt, combined with the original text, forms a joint condition that is then injected into a frozen video DiT backbone via our lightweight and parameter-efficient SSG-Adapter. This unique design, featuring a dual-branch attention mechanism, allows the model to simultaneously harness its powerful generative priors while being precisely steered by external spatial signals. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SSG-DiT achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing models on multiple key metrics in the VBench benchmark, particularly in spatial relationship control and overall consistency.

organization_info.name: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

title: SSG-Dit: A Spatial Signal Guided Framework for Controllable Video Generation

abstract:
Human-centric motion control in video generation remains a critical
challenge, particularly when jointly controlling camera movements and human
poses in scenarios like the iconic Grammy Glambot moment. While recent video
diffusion models have made significant progress, existing approaches struggle
with limited motion representations and inadequate integration of camera and
human motion controls. In this work, we present TokenMotion, the first
DiT-based video diffusion framework that enables fine-grained control over
camera motion, human motion, and their joint interaction. We represent camera
trajectories and human poses as spatio-temporal tokens to enable local control
granularity. Our approach introduces a unified modeling framework utilizing a
decouple-and-fuse strategy, bridged by a human-aware dynamic mask that
effectively handles the spatially-and-temporally varying nature of combined
motion signals. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate TokenMotion's
effectiveness across both text-to-video and image-to-video paradigms,
consistently outperforming current state-of-the-art methods in human-centric
motion control tasks. Our work represents a significant advancement in
controllable video generation, with particular relevance for creative
production applications.

keyInsights: Decoupling camera and human motion signals during processing, then selectively fusing them, prevents interference and maintains coherence for complex motion control.
A dynamic masking mechanism, informed by learned parameters and human pose priors, allows for precise spatial application of different motion types.
Representing camera motion via Plücker coordinates and human poses via DWPose keypoints provides granular and accurate control over each motion type.
originalProblem: Precise control over both camera movement and human pose dynamics simultaneously in video generation is challenging for existing methods.
Current approaches typically handle camera or human motion separately, often failing to coordinate both without motion conflicts, quality degradation, or limited granularity.
Integrating complex motion control into Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based video models, which offer superior scalability, requires specific architectural innovations.
results: Achieves superior quantitative performance over state-of-the-art methods, with higher FID/FVD for visual quality and improved metrics for camera motion accuracy and human pose alignment (lower trajectory deviation, superior PoseErr/DetErr).
Capable of generating videos with precise, complex combined camera and human motion control while maintaining high visual fidelity and temporal consistency.
Demonstrates versatility across diverse human activities and subtle movements, enabling applications in virtual cinematography, motion design, and content creation.
solution: Introduces a token disentanglement architecture that processes camera and human motion as separate spatio-temporal tokens.
Employs a "Decouple-and-Fuse" strategy with parallel encoders and 3D attention blocks for camera and human motion, followed by selective integration.
Utilizes a human-aware dynamic mask, combining learned parameters with human pose priors, to selectively apply motion effects to relevant regions.
paper_summary.summary: TokenMotion, developed by OPPO US AI Center, presents a framework for video generation that provides fine-grained control over both camera movement and human pose dynamics. It achieves this by decoupling and selectively fusing spatio-temporal tokens for camera and human motion, demonstrating improved visual quality and motion fidelity in diverse generation scenarios.

title: TokenMotion: Decoupled Motion Control via Token Disentanglement for  Human-centric Video Generation

abstract: Image-to-video generation has made remarkable progress with the advancements in diffusion models, yet generating videos with realistic motion remains highly challenging. This difficulty arises from the complexity of accurately modeling motion, which involves capturing physical constraints, object interactions, and domain-specific dynamics that are not easily generalized across diverse scenarios. To address this, we propose MotionRAG, a retrieval-augmented framework that enhances motion realism by adapting motion priors from relevant reference videos through Context-Aware Motion Adaptation (CAMA). The key technical innovations include: (i) a retrieval-based pipeline extracting high-level motion features using video encoder and specialized resamplers to distill semantic motion representations; (ii) an in-context learning approach for motion adaptation implemented through a causal transformer architecture; (iii) an attention-based motion injection adapter that seamlessly integrates transferred motion features into pretrained video diffusion models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves significant improvements across multiple domains and various base models, all with negligible computational overhead during inference. Furthermore, our modular design enables zero-shot generalization to new domains by simply updating the retrieval database without retraining any components. This research enhances the core capability of video generation systems by enabling the effective retrieval and transfer of motion priors, facilitating the synthesis of realistic motion dynamics.

organization_info.name: State Key Laboratory for Novel Software Technology, Nanjing University

keyInsights: Explicitly leveraging real-world motion patterns via a retrieval mechanism can significantly enhance motion realism and physical plausibility in image-to-video generation.
In-context learning with a causal transformer provides an efficient and effective way to transfer and adapt abstract, high-level motion features across diverse visual contexts and domains.

A modular, plug-and-play design allows MotionRAG to enhance existing image-to-video diffusion models with minimal computational overhead and enables zero-shot generalization to new domains by merely updating the retrieval database.
originalProblem: Existing image-to-video generation models often struggle to produce physically plausible, semantically coherent, and realistic motion in generated videos.
Current methods rely on implicitly learning motion from data or using text prompts for guidance, which often lack the precision needed for complex temporal dynamics.
Many motion customization techniques require expensive fine-tuning for each specific motion or domain, hindering efficient generalization and scalability.
results: MotionRAG consistently improved performance on OpenVid-1K, achieving up to an 8.6-point increase in Action Score and an 22.0% reduction in FVD for DynamiCrafter, indicating superior motion correctness and overall video quality.

The framework demonstrated strong zero-shot generalization to the specialized SkillVid dataset, improving the Action score by 2.0 points for CogVideoX and reducing FVD by 9.4% for CogVideoX and 17.6% for DynamiCrafter without any fine-tuning.

The Context-Aware Motion Adaptation (CAMA) module was critical, showing robustness even with noisy retrieval (Action score 60.7 vs. baseline 53.5) and adding negligible inference time (0.01-0.06 minutes per video).
solution: MotionRAG employs a three-stage framework: Text-based Video Retrieval, Context-Aware Motion Adaptation (CAMA), and Motion-Guided Video Generation.
CAMA, using a causal transformer, extracts and adapts high-level semantic motion features from retrieved videos to a target image through in-context learning, without per-instance fine-tuning.

A lightweight 'Motion-Adapter' module integrates the adapted motion features into pre-trained image-to-video diffusion models, acting as an additional conditioning signal to guide the denoising process.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Nanjing University and Shanghai AI Laboratory introduced MotionRAG, a retrieval-augmented generation framework that enhances image-to-video synthesis by explicitly leveraging real-world motion priors. The method achieved significant improvements in motion realism, with up to an 8.6-point gain in Action Score and a 22.0% reduction in FVD on general video datasets, while maintaining low computational overhead.

title: MotionRAG: Motion Retrieval-Augmented Image-to-Video Generation

abstract: Generating interaction-centric videos, such as those depicting humans or robots interacting with objects, is crucial for embodied intelligence, as they provide rich and diverse visual priors for robot learning, manipulation policy training, and affordance reasoning. However, existing methods often struggle to model such complex and dynamic interactions. While recent studies show that masks can serve as effective control signals and enhance generation quality, obtaining dense and precise mask annotations remains a major challenge for real-world use. To overcome this limitation, we introduce Mask2IV, a novel framework specifically designed for interaction-centric video generation. It adopts a decoupled two-stage pipeline that first predicts plausible motion trajectories for both actor and object, then generates a video conditioned on these trajectories. This design eliminates the need for dense mask inputs from users while preserving the flexibility to manipulate the interaction process. Furthermore, Mask2IV supports versatile and intuitive control, allowing users to specify the target object of interaction and guide the motion trajectory through action descriptions or spatial position cues. To support systematic training and evaluation, we curate two benchmarks covering diverse action and object categories across both human-object interaction and robotic manipulation scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior visual realism and controllability compared to existing baselines.
title: Mask2IV: Interaction-Centric Video Generation via Mask Trajectories

abstract:
Modern text-to-video synthesis models demonstrate coherent, photorealistic
generation of complex videos from a text description. However, most existing
models lack fine-grained control over camera movement, which is critical for
downstream applications related to content creation, visual effects, and 3D
vision. Recently, new methods demonstrate the ability to generate videos with
controllable camera poses these techniques leverage pre-trained U-Net-based
diffusion models that explicitly disentangle spatial and temporal generation.
Still, no existing approach enables camera control for new, transformer-based
video diffusion models that process spatial and temporal information jointly.
Here, we propose to tame video transformers for 3D camera control using a
ControlNet-like conditioning mechanism that incorporates spatiotemporal camera
embeddings based on Pl\"ucker coordinates. The approach demonstrates
state-of-the-art performance for controllable video generation after
fine-tuning on the RealEstate10K dataset. To the best of our knowledge, our
work is the first to enable camera control for transformer-based video
diffusion models.
title: VD3D: Taming Large Video Diffusion Transformers for 3D Camera Control

abstract: World models based on video generation demonstrate remarkable potential for simulating interactive environments but face persistent difficulties in two key areas: maintaining long-term content consistency when scenes are revisited and enabling precise camera control from user-provided inputs. Existing methods based on explicit 3D reconstruction often compromise flexibility in unbounded scenarios and fine-grained structures. Alternative methods rely directly on previously generated frames without establishing explicit spatial correspondence, thereby constraining controllability and consistency. To address these limitations, we present UCM, a novel framework that unifies long-term memory and precise camera control via a time-aware positional encoding warping mechanism. To reduce computational overhead, we design an efficient dual-stream diffusion transformer for high-fidelity generation. Moreover, we introduce a scalable data curation strategy utilizing point-cloud-based rendering to simulate scene revisiting, facilitating training on over 500K monocular videos. Extensive experiments on real-world and synthetic benchmarks demonstrate that UCM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in long-term scene consistency, while also achieving precise camera controllability in high-fidelity video generation.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Explicit, token-level spatio-temporal correspondence established through time-aware positional encoding warping effectively unifies camera control and long-term memory in world models.
An efficient dual-stream Diffusion Transformer with block-sparse attention is essential for managing the quadratic complexity of attention mechanisms when integrating extensive memory frames.
Simulating diverse multi-view, long-term revisit scenarios from monocular videos via 3D reconstruction provides a scalable solution to dataset scarcity, enhancing model generalization.
originalProblem: Existing video-generation-based world models struggle to maintain long-term content consistency, leading to visual and geometric incoherence upon revisiting scenes.
Achieving precise, user-guided camera control in video generation models is challenging due to inherent viewpoint diversity and complex 3D modeling from 2D observations.
Prior approaches either sacrifice flexibility and detail for explicit 3D reconstruction or lack robust spatial correspondence with implicit 3D priors.
results: Achieves superior camera control, demonstrated by the lowest Rotational Error of 1.01° and competitive Translation Error of 0.11 across benchmarks, surpassing implicit 3D methods.
Establishes outstanding long-term memory capabilities, yielding the lowest FVD (61.47) and LPIPS (0.09) and highest SSIM (0.77) on cycle trajectory evaluations, ensuring consistent scene revisits.

The efficient dual-stream architecture with block-sparse attention enables practical generation speeds of 2.4 seconds per frame with 20 memory frames, and ablation studies confirm the importance of the data curation strategy for robust performance.
solution: Introduces Time-aware Positional Encoding Warping to establish explicit spatio-temporal correspondence by projecting 3D point clouds from memory frames into target viewpoints and augmenting with temporal indices.

Utilizes an efficient dual-stream Diffusion Transformer architecture with block-sparse attention, processing clean and noisy tokens separately to manage computational complexity when incorporating extensive memory.
Proposes a scalable data curation strategy that leverages monocular videos, performing 3D reconstruction and point-cloud-based rendering from novel viewpoints to simulate long-term scene revisits.
paper_summary.summary: A unified framework, UCM, addresses the challenges of maintaining long-term content consistency and enabling precise camera control within video-generation-based world models. Developed by Tsinghua University, Alibaba's Tongyi Lab, and USTC, UCM achieves a rotational error of 1.01° for camera control and shows leading performance in long-term memory tasks, evidenced by an FVD of 61.47 and SSIM of 0.77 on cycle trajectory benchmarks.

title: UCM: Unifying Camera Control and Memory with Time-aware Positional Encoding Warping for World Models

abstract: The ultimate goal of video generation is to satisfy a fundamental trilemma: achieving high visual quality, maintaining rigorous physical consistency, and enabling precise controllability. While recent models can maintain this balance in simple, isolated scenarios, we observe that this equilibrium is fragile and often breaks down as scene complexity increases (e.g., involving collisions or dense traffic). To address this, we introduce \textbf{Motion Forcing}, a framework designed to stabilize this trilemma even in complex generative tasks. Our key insight is to explicitly decouple physical reasoning from visual synthesis via a hierarchical \textbf{``Point-Shape-Appearance''} paradigm. This approach decomposes generation into verifiable stages: modeling complex dynamics as sparse geometric anchors (\textbf{Point}), expanding them into dynamic depth maps that explicitly resolve 3D geometry (\textbf{Shape}), and finally rendering high-fidelity textures (\textbf{Appearance}). Furthermore, to foster robust physical understanding, we employ a \textbf{Masked Point Recovery} strategy. By randomly masking input anchors during training and enforcing the reconstruction of complete dynamic depth, the model is compelled to move beyond passive pattern matching and learn latent physical laws (e.g., inertia) to infer missing trajectories. Extensive experiments on autonomous driving benchmarks show that Motion Forcing significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, maintaining trilemma stability across complex scenes. Evaluations on physics and robotics further confirm our framework's generality.

keyInsights: Explicitly decoupling physical reasoning from visual synthesis allows for a robust hierarchical generation process, establishing a physically sound structural layout before synthesizing visual details.
Dense, temporally-consistent depth maps serve as an effective, physically grounded intermediate representation, bridging the gap between sparse motion control and dense pixel-level video.

Stochastic mode-switching and shared latent representations within a unified diffusion model enable a single network to perform both physics-aware structural inference and high-fidelity appearance synthesis.
Systematic corruption of input motion controls during training forces the model to actively infer latent physical laws, enhancing its robustness to incomplete or ambiguous dynamic inputs.
originalProblem: Existing video generation models struggle to simultaneously achieve high visual quality, rigorous physical consistency, and precise controllability, especially in complex scenarios like object collisions or dense traffic.
End-to-end models entangle dynamics and appearance, often prioritizing easier-to-optimize visual details over long-term physical consistency (e.g., inertia, collision physics).
A significant domain gap exists between sparse control signals (like optical flow) and dense video generation, leading to degraded controllability in intricate scenes.
results: Motion Forcing achieved an FVMD of 205.2 and a Physics-IQ score of 33.2 on the Waymo dataset, demonstrating superior temporal motion coherence and physical plausibility over existing models.

The framework successfully generalized to various physical interaction scenarios, producing plausible collision dynamics on the Physion dataset and guiding robotic arm motions on the Jaco Play dataset.
Ablation studies confirmed that the intermediate depth representation and spatially explicit depth warping for ego-motion encoding are crucial for robust motion coherence and physical reasoning.
solution: The Motion Forcing framework proposes a decoupled, hierarchical "Point-Shape-Appearance" paradigm, progressing from sparse control points to dense depth maps, then to final RGB video.
A unified 3D Diffusion Transformer backbone with dual independent diffusion timesteps and Adaptive Layer Normalization is used to simultaneously handle physical reasoning and neural rendering.

A "forcing strategy" (stochastic mode-switching) during training compels the model to learn physics engine capabilities (Mode I: Point+Camera to Depth) and neural rendering (Mode II: Depth to Appearance).
A Masked Point Recovery strategy during training systematically corrupts input motion controls to foster active physical reasoning by inferring missing trajectories and geometries.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology introduce Motion Forcing, a decoupled framework for video generation that prioritizes physical consistency and controllability by separating physical reasoning from visual synthesis. The framework achieves superior motion coherence (FVMD 205.2) and physical plausibility (Physics-IQ 33.2) on the Waymo dataset, along with strong generalization across diverse physical interaction scenarios.

title: Motion Forcing: A Decoupled Framework for Robust Video Generation in Motion Dynamics

abstract:
Camera control is crucial for generating expressive and cinematic videos.
Existing methods rely on explicit sequences of camera parameters as control
conditions, which can be cumbersome for users to construct, particularly for
intricate camera movements. To provide a more intuitive camera control method,
we propose CamCloneMaster, a framework that enables users to replicate camera
movements from reference videos without requiring camera parameters or
test-time fine-tuning. CamCloneMaster seamlessly supports reference-based
camera control for both Image-to-Video and Video-to-Video tasks within a
unified framework. Furthermore, we present the Camera Clone Dataset, a
large-scale synthetic dataset designed for camera clone learning, encompassing
diverse scenes, subjects, and camera movements. Extensive experiments and user
studies demonstrate that CamCloneMaster outperforms existing methods in terms
of both camera controllability and visual quality.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Frame concatenation across all transformer layers proves more effective than channel concatenation or ControlNet-like architectures for learning global camera motion properties
Ground truth camera parameters significantly outperform pose estimation methods, validating the need for direct reference-based approaches over parameter estimation pipelines
Selective fine-tuning of spatial-temporal attention layers achieves better camera accuracy and visual quality compared to full model fine-tuning
Synthetic dataset generation enables precise control over camera trajectories and scene pairing that would be prohibitively difficult to capture in real-world settings
originalProblem: Existing camera-controllable video generation methods rely on explicit camera parameters that are difficult for users to specify and often inaccurate when estimated from reference videos
Parameter-free approaches suffer from limited generalization, computational overhead from inversion processes, and vulnerability to unreliable priors
Lack of large-scale datasets with paired videos containing identical camera trajectories needed for training robust camera clone models
Most methods specialize in either Image-to-Video or Video-to-Video tasks rather than providing unified frameworks
results: Achieves state-of-the-art camera accuracy with 1.49 rotation error and 2.37 translation error, outperforming CameraCtrl (2.05, 3.12) and CamI2V (2.17, 3.54)
Demonstrates superior visual quality metrics including highest CLIP Score (31.12) and lowest FVD (171.39) compared to all baselines
User studies show 81-88% preference over baselines for I2V tasks and 78-91% preference for V2V tasks across camera accuracy, video-text consistency, and temporal consistency
Successfully handles complex camera movements like "Boom up + Dolly in" and "Tilt up + Dolly in" that challenge existing methods
solution: Direct reference-based camera control using token concatenation along the frame dimension to inject camera motion and content reference videos into transformer-based latent diffusion model
Unified framework supporting both I2V and V2V generation by replacing content reference with zero latent for image input
Selective fine-tuning of only 3D spatio-temporal attention layers while freezing other parameters to preserve generative capabilities
Construction of Camera Clone Dataset with 391K synthetic videos and 1.155M triple video sets rendered in Unreal Engine 5 with precise camera trajectory control
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from CUHK, Zhejiang University, and Kuaishou Technology develop CamCloneMaster, a transformer-based diffusion framework that enables users to replicate camera movements from reference videos without requiring explicit camera parameters or pose estimation, achieving superior performance over parameter-based methods (CameraCtrl, CamI2V) and training-free approaches (MotionClone) with 1.49 rotation error and 2.37 translation error on camera accuracy metrics while supporting both Image-to-Video and Video-to-Video generation through a unified token concatenation architecture that injects reference camera motion and content videos directly into the latent diffusion process, trained on a custom Camera Clone Dataset of 391K synthetic videos rendered in Unreal Engine 5 across 40 diverse 3D scenes with precisely controlled camera trajectories, demonstrating 81-88% user preference over baselines in subjective evaluations and validating the core motivation that even ground truth camera parameters outperform MegaSaM-estimated parameters by 76-87% in user studies, though the approach incurs increased computational overhead from concatenating reference tokens along the frame dimension.

title: CamCloneMaster: Enabling Reference-based Camera Control for Video  Generation

abstract:
Motions in a video primarily consist of camera motion, induced by camera
movement, and object motion, resulting from object movement. Accurate control
of both camera and object motion is essential for video generation. However,
existing works either mainly focus on one type of motion or do not clearly
distinguish between the two, limiting their control capabilities and diversity.
Therefore, this paper presents MotionCtrl, a unified and flexible motion
controller for video generation designed to effectively and independently
control camera and object motion. The architecture and training strategy of
MotionCtrl are carefully devised, taking into account the inherent properties
of camera motion, object motion, and imperfect training data. Compared to
previous methods, MotionCtrl offers three main advantages: 1) It effectively
and independently controls camera motion and object motion, enabling more
fine-grained motion control and facilitating flexible and diverse combinations
of both types of motion. 2) Its motion conditions are determined by camera
poses and trajectories, which are appearance-free and minimally impact the
appearance or shape of objects in generated videos. 3) It is a relatively
