generalizable model that can adapt to a wide array of camera poses and
trajectories once trained. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments
have been conducted to demonstrate the superiority of MotionCtrl over existing
methods. Project Page: https://wzhouxiff.github.io/projects/MotionCtrl/

keyInsights: Explicitly disentangling camera and object motion via dedicated modules within a unified framework enables superior and fine-grained control over video generation.

Integrating global camera motion control with temporal transformers and local object motion control with spatial convolutional layers provides an effective architectural design for distinct motion types.

A multi-step training strategy, leveraging synthesized captions and trajectories, effectively addresses data scarcity by first learning camera motion and then object motion using a dense-to-sparse trajectory approach.
originalProblem: Existing controllable video generation methods often fail to disentangle camera motion from object motion, limiting fine-grained control.
Many prior approaches require retraining or fine-tuning for each new motion template, hindering flexibility and generalizability.
Some motion conditions used in previous methods are appearance-dependent, leading to unnatural object appearances in generated videos.
results: MotionCtrl achieves superior control efficacy, with a CamMC score of 0.0289 for basic camera poses (versus AnimateDiff's 0.0548) and an ObjMC score of 28.877 for object motion (versus VideoComposer's 36.8351).
The framework maintains high video quality and text alignment, recording better FID (124.09), FVD (852.15), and CLIPSIM (0.2319) scores compared to baseline models.

Ablation studies confirm that integrating CMCM with temporal transformers and training OMCM with a dense-then-sparse trajectory strategy are optimal, validating the architectural and training design choices.
solution: MotionCtrl extends Latent Video Diffusion Models with a Camera Motion Control Module (CMCM) and an Object Motion Control Module (OMCM) for disentangled control.
CMCM uses camera poses (rotation and translation matrices) and integrates with the LVDM's temporal transformers for global camera motion control.

OMCM uses 2D displacement vectors for object trajectories and integrates with the LVDM's convolutional layers for local object motion control, employing a multi-step training strategy with augmented datasets.
paper_summary.summary: MotionCtrl introduces a framework that achieves unified and flexible control over video generation by independently managing camera and object motion. The system integrates specialized modules into latent video diffusion models, yielding more natural videos with precise motion control and enhanced text-to-video alignment.

title:
MotionCtrl: A Unified and Flexible Motion Controller for Video
  Generation

abstract: Motion-controllable video generation is crucial for egocentric applications in virtual reality and embodied AI. However, existing methods often struggle to achieve 3D-consistent fine-grained hand articulation. By adopting on 2D trajectories or implicit poses, they collapse 3D geometry into spatially ambiguous signals or over rely on human-centric priors. Under severe egocentric occlusions, this causes motion inconsistencies and hallucinated artifacts, as well as preventing cross-embodiment generalization to robotic hands. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework that generates egocentric videos from a single reference frame, leveraging sparse 3D hand joints as embodiment-agnostic control signals with clear semantic and geometric structures. We introduce an efficient control module that resolves occlusion ambiguities while fully preserving 3D information. Specifically, it extracts occlusion-aware features from the source reference frame by penalizing unreliable visual signals from hidden joints, and employs a 3D-based weighting mechanism to robustly handle dynamically occluded target joints during motion propagation. Concurrently, the module directly injects 3D geometric embeddings into the latent space to strictly enforce structural consistency. To facilitate robust training and evaluation, we develop an automated annotation pipeline that yields over one million high-quality egocentric video clips paired with precise hand trajectories. Additionally, we register humanoid kinematic and camera data to construct a cross-embodiment benchmark. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, generating high-fidelity egocentric videos with realistic interactions and exhibiting exceptional cross-embodiment generalization to robotic hands.

keyInsights: Sparse 3D hand joints provide explicit 3D geometry and semantic identity, effectively resolving occlusion ambiguities and enabling precise, fine-grained control over hand articulation.
An embodiment-agnostic control representation allows a single model to generalize effectively across diverse human and robotic hand structures without extensive domain-specific adaptation.

Separately handling occlusions in source feature extraction and target feature propagation, combined with explicit 3D geometric embeddings, is crucial for generating high-fidelity and structurally consistent egocentric videos.
originalProblem: Existing egocentric video generation methods struggle to maintain fine-grained 3D-consistent hand articulation and precise motion control, especially under severe occlusions.
2D track-based and pose-based control signals lead to loss of 3D structural integrity, depth ambiguity, and a lack of cross-embodiment generalization for robotic hands.
There is a lack of large-scale, accurately annotated egocentric datasets with 3D hand trajectories for both human and robotic domains.
results: The method achieved superior quantitative performance on human hand datasets (Ego4D, EgoDex), with up to a 68% reduction in MPJPE compared to state-of-the-art 2D track-based methods.

A unified model demonstrated robust cross-embodiment generalization, effectively controlling robotic hands (G1-Dex3-1, H1-Inspire) and showing significantly improved FVD and MPJPE compared to baseline methods.

User studies confirmed a preference for the proposed method in both visual quality and motion accuracy, while ablation studies validated the individual contributions of occlusion handling and 3D geometric embeddings.
solution: The framework leverages sparse 3D hand joints as explicit control signals, integrated into a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture, specifically the WAN 2.1 framework.

An occlusion-aware conditioning mechanism is introduced, featuring occlusion-removed context aggregation for source frames and occlusion-aware feature propagation to target frames using a differentiable Z-buffer.

3D geometric embeddings, combining projected 3D coordinates and learnable joint identities, are explicitly injected to preserve depth and semantic information, processed by a causal 3D convolutional head for temporal consistency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ETH Zurich developed a framework for controllable egocentric video generation that utilizes occlusion-aware sparse 3D hand joints as control signals, achieving fine-grained articulation and cross-embodiment generalization. The method significantly outperformed existing 2D-based approaches, showing up to a 68% reduction in MPJPE on the EgoDex dataset for human hands and substantial FVD improvements for robotic hands.

title: Controllable Egocentric Video Generation via Occlusion-Aware Sparse 3D Hand Joints

abstract: Controlling the movements of dynamic objects and the camera within generated videos is a meaningful yet challenging task. Due to the lack of datasets with comprehensive 6D pose annotations, existing text-to-video methods can not simultaneously control the motions of both camera and objects in 3D-aware manner, resulting in limited controllability over generated contents. To address this issue and facilitate the research in this field, we introduce a Synthetic Dataset for Free-Form Motion Control (SynFMC). The proposed SynFMC dataset includes diverse object and environment categories and covers various motion patterns according to specific rules, simulating common and complex real-world scenarios. The complete 6D pose information facilitates models learning to disentangle the motion effects from objects and the camera in a video.~To provide precise 3D-aware motion control, we further propose a method trained on SynFMC, Free-Form Motion Control (FMC). FMC can control the 6D poses of objects and camera independently or simultaneously, producing high-fidelity videos. Moreover, it is compatible with various personalized text-to-image (T2I) models for different content styles. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed FMC outperforms previous methods across multiple scenarios.
title: Free-Form Motion Control: Controlling the 6D Poses of Camera and Objects in Video Generation

abstract: Humans excel at forecasting the future dynamics of a scene given just a single image. Video generation models that can mimic this ability are an essential component for intelligent systems. Recent approaches have improved temporal coherence and 3D consistency in single-image-conditioned video generation. However, these methods often lack robust user controllability, such as modifying the camera path, limiting their applicability in real-world applications. Most existing camera-controlled image-to-video models struggle with accurately modeling camera motion, maintaining temporal consistency, and preserving geometric integrity. Leveraging explicit intermediate 3D representations offers a promising solution by enabling coherent video generation aligned with a given camera trajectory. Although these methods often use 3D point clouds to render scenes and introduce object motion in a later stage, this two-step process still falls short in achieving full temporal consistency, despite allowing precise control over camera movement. We propose a novel framework that constructs a 3D Gaussian scene representation and samples plausible object motion, given a single image in a single forward pass. This enables fast, camera-guided video generation without the need for iterative denoising to inject object motion into render frames. Extensive experiments on the KITTI, Waymo, RealEstate10K and DL3DV-10K datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art video quality and inference efficiency. The project page is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: An explicit dynamic 4D Gaussian splatting representation provides a robust and efficient way to model complex, dynamic scenes, inherently ensuring temporal and geometric consistency.

Adopting a generative approach to predict object velocities from a single image, rather than a deterministic one, better captures the inherent uncertainty of future motion, leading to more plausible dynamics.
Fusing features from large-scale self-supervised models like DINOv2 significantly enhances the semantic understanding and detail preservation during 4D reconstruction and video generation tasks.
originalProblem: Existing camera-controlled image-to-video (I2V) models often struggle with maintaining temporal coherence, geometric consistency, and accurate camera motion adherence, especially with complex trajectories.
Many I2V approaches are computationally expensive, relying on iterative denoising or 2D processing, leading to slow inference and lacking robust 3D understanding.
Single-image-to-4D methods are typically restricted to static scenes or single objects, often failing to handle complex, large-scale dynamic environments with multiple moving elements.
results: Pixel-to-4D consistently outperformed four state-of-the-art camera-guided I2V models across KITTI, Waymo, RealEstate10K, and DL3DV-10K datasets, achieving superior PSNR, LPIPS, SSIM, and FVD scores.

On the Waymo dataset, the method achieved an FVD of 30.9 (compared to MotionCtrl's 43.3) and a PSNR of 19.4 (vs. RealCam-I2V's 17.0), while demonstrating significantly faster inference times (e.g., 5.9 seconds per video on KITTI).

Ablation studies confirmed that dynamic modeling with generative velocities, the use of multiple Gaussian splats per pixel, and the integration of DINOv2 features are crucial for achieving high-quality and consistent 4D scene generation.
solution: The Pixel-to-4D framework predicts a dynamic 4D scene representation from a single input image in a single feedforward pass, leveraging spatio-temporal parameters for 3D Gaussian splats.

It employs an end-to-end trainable neural network that integrates DINOv2 features for semantic context and uses a generative approach, sampling latent noise, to model inherently uncertain object motion.
The model is trained with a composite loss function, including LPIPS and KL divergence, in a two-stage process to optimize both static and dynamic Gaussian components.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Glasgow developed Pixel-to-4D, a framework that generates dynamic 4D scene representations from a single image for camera-controlled video generation. The method consistently outperformed existing image-to-video models across four datasets, achieving an FVD of 30.9 on Waymo and significantly faster inference times of 5.9 seconds per video on KITTI.

title: Pixel-to-4D: Camera-Controlled Image-to-Video Generation with Dynamic 3D Gaussians

abstract: Controllable image-to-video (I2V) generation transforms a reference image into a coherent video guided by user-specified control signals. In content creation workflows, precise and simultaneous control over camera motion, object motion, and lighting direction enhances both accuracy and flexibility. However, existing approaches typically treat these control signals separately, largely due to the scarcity of datasets with high-quality joint annotations and mismatched control spaces across modalities. We present VidCRAFT3, a unified and flexible I2V framework that supports both independent and joint control over camera motion, object motion, and lighting direction by integrating three core components. Image2Cloud reconstructs a 3D point cloud from the reference image to enable precise camera motion control. ObjMotionNet encodes sparse object trajectories into multi-scale optical flow features to guide object motion. The Spatial Triple-Attention Transformer integrates lighting direction embeddings via parallel cross-attention. To address the scarcity of jointly annotated data, we curate the VideoLightingDirection (VLD) dataset of synthetic static-scene video clips with per-frame lighting-direction labels, and adopt a three-stage training strategy that enables robust learning without fully joint annotations. Extensive experiments show that VidCRAFT3 outperforms existing methods in control precision and visual coherence. Code and data will be released. Project page: this https URL.
title: VidCRAFT3: Camera, Object, and Lighting Control for Image-to-Video Generation

abstract: This paper aims to manipulate multi-entity 3D motions in video generation. Previous methods on controllable video generation primarily leverage 2D control signals to manipulate object motions and have achieved remarkable synthesis results. However, 2D control signals are inherently limited in expressing the 3D nature of object motions. To overcome this problem, we introduce 3DTrajMaster, a robust controller that regulates multi-entity dynamics in 3D space, given user-desired 6DoF pose (location and rotation) sequences of entities. At the core of our approach is a plug-and-play 3D-motion grounded object injector that fuses multiple input entities with their respective 3D trajectories through a gated self-attention mechanism. In addition, we exploit an injector architecture to preserve the video diffusion prior, which is crucial for generalization ability. To mitigate video quality degradation, we introduce a domain adaptor during training and employ an annealed sampling strategy during inference. To address the lack of suitable training data, we construct a 360-Motion Dataset, which first correlates collected 3D human and animal assets with GPT-generated trajectory and then captures their motion with 12 evenly-surround cameras on diverse 3D UE platforms. Extensive experiments show that 3DTrajMaster sets a new state-of-the-art in both accuracy and generalization for controlling multi-entity 3D motions. Project page: this http URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: The plug-and-play architecture with gated self-attention effectively integrates complex 3D motion conditions into pre-trained video diffusion models without degrading their prior, ensuring strong generalization.
Training on a carefully constructed synthetic dataset combined with a LoRA-based domain adaptor is crucial for bridging the synthetic-to-real domain gap and preventing quality degradation.
An annealed sampling strategy, where trajectory guidance is reduced in later diffusion steps, balances precise 3D control with the base model's high-fidelity prior, optimizing overall video quality.
originalProblem: Existing 2D control signals for video generation struggle to accurately represent the inherent 3D nature of real-world object motion, making precise 3D rotation and inter-object occlusion difficult to manage.
Prior 3D-aware video generation methods primarily focus on camera motion control, lacking the ability to customize individual object motions in 3D space, especially for multiple interacting entities.
There is a scarcity of high-quality, diverse training datasets that include paired 3D entity descriptions and accurate 6DoF trajectories for general non-rigid objects.
results: 3DTrajMaster achieved a translational error (TransErr) of 0.398 meters and a rotational error (RotErr) of 0.277 degrees for all entities, representing a 4-5x improvement in translational accuracy and 3-7x improvement in rotational accuracy compared to 2D baselines.

The model successfully generates videos with granular 6DoF control over multiple entities, accurately handling complex motions like 180° turn-backs and resolving 3D occlusions, which are challenging for 2D approaches.

Qualitative and quantitative evaluations (FVD 1546.15, FID 96.75) confirm high-fidelity video generation and robust generalization across diverse entity categories, backgrounds, and fine-grained entity editing capabilities.
solution: A plug-and-play 3D-Motion Grounded Object Injector is developed, which uses entity-wise addition to bond encoded text descriptions with learnable 6DoF trajectory embeddings, fusing them into the video latent via a gated self-attention layer.

A synthetic 360°-Motion Dataset is constructed using Unreal Engine, featuring 70 animated 3D assets, GPT-generated 3D spline trajectories, and 360-degree camera views to provide accurate 6DoF ground truth for multi-entity scenes.

A LoRA-based Video Domain Adaptor and an annealed sampling strategy are employed to mitigate domain shift from synthetic data, preserving the generalization ability and high quality of the base video diffusion model during inference.
paper_summary.summary: 3DTrajMaster introduces the first method for controlling 6 Degrees of Freedom (6DoF) multi-entity motion in 3D space for video generation. This framework achieves up to a 7x improvement in rotational accuracy and a 5x improvement in translational accuracy over 2D baselines, while maintaining high video fidelity.

title: 3DTrajMaster: Mastering 3D Trajectory for Multi-Entity Motion in Video Generation

abstract: We propose PostCam, a framework for novel-view video generation that enables post-capture editing of camera trajectories in dynamic scenes. We find that existing video recapture methods suffer from suboptimal camera motion injection strategies; such suboptimal designs not only limit camera control precision but also result in generated videos that fail to preserve fine visual details from the source video. To achieve more accurate and flexible motion manipulation, PostCam introduces a query-shared cross-attention module. It integrates two distinct forms of control signals: the 6-DoF camera poses and the 2D rendered video frames. By fusing them into a unified representation within a shared feature space, our model can extract underlying motion cues, which enhances both control precision and generation quality. Furthermore, we adopt a two-stage training strategy: the model first learns coarse camera control from pose inputs, and then incorporates visual information to refine motion accuracy and enhance visual fidelity. Experiments on both real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrate that PostCam outperforms state-of-the-art methods by over 20% in camera control precision and view consistency, while achieving the highest video generation quality. Our project webpage is publicly available at: this https URL

organization_info.name: Shanghai InSpatio Intelligent Technology Co., Ltd.

keyInsights: Directly conditioning generative models on abstract numerical camera poses alone leads to weak cross-modal alignment and limited control precision.
Relying solely on visual rendering cues (derived from 3D reconstruction) for camera control is vulnerable to errors in depth estimation, which can propagate as severe visual artifacts.

A shared query mechanism across both abstract poses and visual renders allows for effective distillation of common camera motion cues, simultaneously improving control accuracy and preserving visual detail.
originalProblem: Existing video "recapture" methods struggle to accurately inject camera motion signals, leading to imprecise trajectory following.
Current techniques often lose fine visual details and subject identity from the source video, resulting in blurring or "identity drift" in generated outputs.
Ensuring robust spatiotemporal consistency and plausibly inpainting unobserved regions in dynamic scenes remains a challenge for novel-view video generation.
results: PostCam demonstrated over 20% improvement in camera control precision, achieving lower Rotation Error (0.0501 vs 0.0649) and Translation Error (0.1021 vs 0.1480) on the OpenViD dataset compared to state-of-the-art methods.

The method achieved higher video generation quality, scoring best on VBench metrics for aesthetic quality, image quality, motion smoothness, and subject/background consistency, alongside lower FID (67.20 vs 85.68) and FVD scores.

Qualitative comparisons showed superior preservation of fine visual details, maintenance of subject identity, and robustness in challenging dynamic scenes where other advanced methods failed or produced significant artifacts.
solution: A Query-Shared Cross-Attention module effectively fuses heterogeneous camera conditions, combining 6-DoF camera poses with downsampled rendered proxy videos.
A two-stage training strategy first enables coarse motion learning from poses, then refines motion accuracy and visual fidelity using rendered video guidance.
The system extends a state-of-the-art video diffusion model (Wan2.1) by injecting source video latents to maintain content consistency while selectively training core modules.
paper_summary.summary: PostCam provides a framework for camera-controllable novel-view video generation, enabling post-capture editing of camera trajectories in dynamic scenes by fusing 6-DoF poses and visual renders through a Query-Shared Cross-Attention module. This approach, from Zhejiang University and Shanghai InSpatio, achieves over 20% greater precision in camera control and superior video quality on the OpenViD dataset compared to prior methods.

title: PostCam: Camera-Controllable Novel-View Video Generation with Query-Shared Cross-Attention

abstract:
We propose a unified framework for motion control in video generation that
seamlessly integrates camera movement, object-level translation, and
fine-grained local motion using trajectory-based inputs. In contrast to prior
methods that address these motion types through separate modules or
task-specific designs, our approach offers a cohesive solution by projecting
user-defined trajectories into the latent space of pre-trained image-to-video
generation models via a lightweight motion injector. Users can specify
keypoints and their motion paths to control localized deformations, entire
object motion, virtual camera dynamics, or combinations of these. The injected
trajectory signals guide the generative process to produce temporally
consistent and semantically aligned motion sequences. Our framework
demonstrates superior performance across multiple video motion control tasks,
including stylized motion effects (e.g., motion brushes), dynamic viewpoint
changes, and precise local motion manipulation. Experiments show that our
method provides significantly better controllability and visual quality
compared to prior approaches and commercial solutions, while remaining broadly
compatible with various state-of-the-art video generation backbones. Project
page: this https URL

keyInsights: Diverse motion types, including camera and object movements, can be effectively unified under a single trajectory instruction paradigm by operating within the latent space of diffusion models.
Injecting features extracted from initial image positions and guided by Gaussian distributions along trajectories offers an efficient and compatible mechanism for motion control in DiT architectures.

Tail Dropout Regularization is crucial for robust performance in practical applications, teaching the model that an early trajectory termination does not necessarily imply occlusion or object disappearance.
originalProblem: Existing methods for controllable video generation suffer from fragmented control, requiring separate modules or specialized designs for camera, object, and local motion.
Users face complex and inefficient workflows when trying to coordinate diverse motion types, limiting creative expression and making advanced video creation cumbersome.
A lack of a consistent and intuitive interface for fine-grained, unified control over various motion elements within AI-generated videos.
results: ATI achieves over 55% accuracy (Acc@0.05) in following user-specified trajectories on a test set, with the Seaweed-7B backbone reaching 59.0% and Wan2.1-14B reaching 55.9%.
Qualitatively demonstrates robust and coherent control over combined camera and object motions, successfully generating complex effects like dolly zooms and managing intersecting trajectories.
Maintains computational efficiency, generating a five-second, 480p video in approximately 8 GPU-minutes, indicating its practical viability for controllable video generation.
solution: ATI proposes a unified trajectory-based framework that represents all forms of motion (local, object, camera) as 2D trajectories of user-defined key points.
The system injects these user-specified trajectories into the latent space of pre-trained image-to-video diffusion models using spatially distributed Gaussian feature masks.
Tail Dropout Regularization is implemented during training to prevent the generation of spurious occluders when user-defined trajectories terminate before the end of the video sequence.
paper_summary.summary: This research from ByteDance Intelligent Creation introduces "ATI," a unified framework for controllable video generation, allowing users to guide camera movement, object translation, and local deformations using a single trajectory-based input. The method achieves quantitative tracking accuracy, with over 55% of frames having point distances within 5% of the image diagonal, and ensures visual quality while maintaining efficient generation times.

title: ATI: Any Trajectory Instruction for Controllable Video Generation

abstract: Human-centric video generation has advanced rapidly, yet existing methods struggle to produce controllable and physically consistent Human-Object Interaction (HOI) videos. Existing works rely on dense control signals, template videos, or carefully crafted text prompts, which limit flexibility and generalization to novel objects. We introduce a framework, namely DISPLAY, guided by Sparse Motion Guidance, composed only of wrist joint coordinates and a shape-agnostic object bounding box. This lightweight guidance alleviates the imbalance between human and object representations and enables intuitive user control. To enhance fidelity under such sparse conditions, we propose an Object-Stressed Attention mechanism that improves object robustness. To address the scarcity of high-quality HOI data, we further develop a Multi-Task Auxiliary Training strategy with a dedicated data curation pipeline, allowing the model to benefit from both reliable HOI samples and auxiliary tasks. Comprehensive experiments show that our method achieves high-fidelity, controllable HOI generation across diverse tasks. The project page can be found at \href{this https URL}.

keyInsights: Sparse motion guidance is effective in balancing human and object representations, allowing for robust and high-fidelity HOI video generation with minimal input, even for novel objects.

Object-Stressed Attention improves the integration of object appearance and motion within the generation process, leading to more realistic and physically consistent hand-object interactions under sparse control.

A multi-task training strategy, leveraging diverse data sources (curated HOI data and weakly-annotated human-centric videos), is crucial for overcoming data scarcity and enhancing the model's generalization across various HOI scenarios.
originalProblem: Generating controllable and physically consistent complex human-object interaction (HOI) videos remains a challenge for existing human-centric video generation methods.
Large video generation models (LVGMs) struggle with fine-grained spatio-temporal control over HOI due to reliance on text prompts, leading to non-deterministic and inconsistent outputs.
Explicit guidance methods suffer from representation imbalance, with strong controls for human hands but weak structural representations for objects, often requiring template videos.
There is a scarcity of high-quality, HOI-annotated video data, which limits the generalization capabilities of training models.
results: DISPLAY achieves superior visual quality (FID 67.501, Aesthetics 0.547) and strong temporal consistency (FVD 560.29) compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
The framework exhibits robust object fidelity, with significantly higher Object-CLIP (0.890) and Object-DINO (0.832) scores, demonstrating effective preservation of object appearance.

The model supports versatile HOI scenarios, including object replacement, insertion, and environmental interaction, and can generate stable long videos (e.g., one minute) through a recursive approach.
solution: Introduces DISPLAY, a DiT-based framework with a ControlNet-style architecture that uses sparse motion guidance, comprising only wrist joint coordinates and a shape-agnostic object bounding box.
Integrates an Object-Stressed Attention mechanism to selectively emphasize object-related tokens, enhancing object robustness and quality within complex HOI regions.
Employs a Multi-Task Auxiliary Training strategy, coupled with a dedicated data curation pipeline, to learn from both reliable HOI samples and supplementary, weakly-annotated human-centric videos.
paper_summary.summary: Baidu Inc. researchers developed DISPLAY, a framework for directable human-object interaction (HOI) video generation, utilizing sparse motion guidance and a multi-task auxiliary training strategy. The system demonstrates superior visual quality (FID 67.501), strong temporal consistency (FVD 560.29), and robust object preservation (Object-CLIP 0.890) compared to leading baselines.

title: DISPLAY: Directable Human-Object Interaction Video Generation via Sparse Motion Guidance and Multi-Task Auxiliary

abstract: Multi-object video motion transfer poses significant challenges for Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures due to inherent motion entanglement and lack of object-level control. We present MultiMotion, a novel unified framework that overcomes these limitations. Our core innovation is Maskaware Attention Motion Flow (AMF), which utilizes SAM2 masks to explicitly disentangle and control motion features for multiple objects within the DiT pipeline. Furthermore, we introduce RectPC, a high-order predictor-corrector solver for efficient and accurate sampling, particularly beneficial for multi-entity generation. To facilitate rigorous evaluation, we construct the first benchmark dataset specifically for DiT-based multi-object motion transfer. MultiMotion demonstrably achieves precise, semantically aligned, and temporally coherent motion transfer for multiple distinct objects, maintaining DiT's high quality and scalability. The code is in the supp.
title: MultiMotion: Multi Subject Video Motion Transfer via Video Diffusion Transformer

abstract: Recent advances in video generation have shown promise for generating future scenarios, critical for planning and control in autonomous driving and embodied intelligence. However, real-world applications demand more than visually plausible predictions; they require reasoning about object motions based on explicitly defined boundary conditions, such as initial scene image and partial object motion. We term this capability Boundary Conditional Motion Reasoning. Current approaches either neglect explicit user-defined motion constraints, producing physically inconsistent motions, or conversely demand complete motion inputs, which are rarely available in practice. Here we introduce Motion Dreamer, a two-stage framework that explicitly separates motion reasoning from visual synthesis, addressing these limitations. Our approach introduces instance flow, a sparse-to-dense motion representation enabling effective integration of partial user-defined motions, and the motion inpainting strategy to robustly enable reasoning motions of other objects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Motion Dreamer significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving superior motion plausibility and visual realism, thus bridging the gap towards practical boundary conditional motion reasoning. Our webpage is available: this https URL.
title: Motion Dreamer: Boundary Conditional Motion Reasoning for Physically Coherent Video Generation

abstract: Text-to-video diffusion models have enabled high-quality video synthesis, yet often fail to generate temporally coherent and physically plausible motion. A key reason is the models' insufficient understanding of complex motions that natural videos often entail. Recent works tackle this problem by aligning diffusion model features with those from pretrained video encoders. However, these encoders mix video appearance and dynamics into entangled features, limiting the benefit of such alignment. In this paper, we propose a motion-centric alignment framework that learns a disentangled motion subspace from a pretrained video encoder. This subspace is optimized to predict ground-truth optical flow, ensuring it captures true motion dynamics. We then align the latent features of a text-to-video diffusion model to this new subspace, enabling the generative model to internalize motion knowledge and generate more plausible videos. Our method improves the physical commonsense in a state-of-the-art video diffusion model, while preserving adherence to textual prompts, as evidenced by empirical evaluations on VideoPhy, VideoPhy2, VBench, and VBench-2.0, along with a user study.

keyInsights: Learning a disentangled, motion-specific representation, explicitly supervised by optical flow and forced through a low-dimensional bottleneck, is crucial for effectively extracting dynamic information.

Soft relational alignment, particularly with a weighted temporal component, provides a stable and effective mechanism for transferring motion understanding without hard feature matching or compromising the base generative model.

Internalizing motion knowledge into the diffusion model's latent space during training eliminates the need for external conditioning or complex simulations at inference time, making the approach practical.
originalProblem: Text-to-video diffusion models frequently generate videos with unnatural, physically implausible motion due to a lack of deep understanding of motion dynamics.
Existing solutions like simulation-based methods are computationally intensive and domain-specific, while conditioning-based methods require additional, often unavailable, inputs at inference time.
Prior representation alignment techniques often entangle static appearance with dynamic motion features, making targeted motion improvement difficult and risking generative model instability.
results: MOALIGN-2B achieved the highest Joint score (24.9) on the VideoPhy2 benchmark, outperforming the base CogVideoX-2B (22.3) and VideoREPA-2B (23.0), demonstrating improved physical plausibility and semantic adherence.

The framework maintained overall generation quality on VBench and showed improvements in 'Commonsense' (65.5) and 'Human Fidelity' (86.7) metrics on VBench-2.0, indicating better instance preservation and dynamic relationships.

Blind user studies confirmed a strong preference for MOALIGN-generated videos, with 68% preferring them over CogVideoX-2B and 78% over VideoREPA-2B, highlighting enhanced perceived realism and prompt fidelity.
solution: A two-stage fine-tuning framework, MOALIGN, is proposed to learn a disentangled motion subspace from a frozen, pretrained video encoder (VideoMAEv2).
Stage one uses a dimensionality bottleneck and optical flow supervision to create low-dimensional, motion-centric features, isolating dynamic information.

Stage two aligns the latent features of a text-to-video diffusion model (CogVideoX-2B) to these motion-centric features using a soft relational alignment loss with weighted temporal similarity, internalizing dynamic structure without compromising stability.
paper_summary.summary: MOALIGN introduces a framework to internalize motion understanding into text-to-video diffusion models by disentangling motion-specific features and using a soft relational alignment strategy. This approach enables generated videos to exhibit greater physical plausibility and temporal coherence, achieving a Joint score of 24.9 on the VideoPhy2 benchmark and preferred by users over baselines in blind studies.

title: MoAlign: Motion-Centric Representation Alignment for Video Diffusion Models

abstract: Motion controllability is crucial in video synthesis. However, most previous methods are limited to single control types, and combining them often results in logical conflicts. In this paper, we propose a disentangled and unified framework, namely I2VControl, to overcome the logical conflicts. We rethink camera control, object dragging, and motion brush, reformulating all tasks into a consistent representation based on point trajectories, each managed by a dedicated formulation. Accordingly, we propose a spatial partitioning strategy, where each unit is assigned to a concomitant control category, enabling diverse control types to be dynamically orchestrated within a single synthesis pipeline without conflicts. Furthermore, we design an adapter structure that functions as a plug-in for pre-trained models and is agnostic to specific model architectures. We conduct extensive experiments, achieving excellent performance on various control tasks, and our method further facilitates user-driven creative combinations, enhancing innovation and creativity. Project page: this https URL .

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Unifying disparate motion control signals into a single dense point trajectory representation effectively resolves logical conflicts arising from combining different control types.
A spatial partitioning strategy that assigns distinct control categories to specific regions within a scene enables the dynamic and simultaneous integration of multiple motion controls.

A lightweight, trainable adapter network can extend existing pre-trained video generation models with comprehensive motion control capabilities without requiring extensive retraining of the foundational model.
originalProblem: Existing video synthesis methods, particularly with diffusion models, often lack precise control over specific motion dynamics in generated content.
Current motion control techniques are typically designed for isolated control types (e.g., camera, object dragging, or local brush), leading to fragmented creative workflows.

Directly combining these single-purpose controls often results in logical conflicts and inconsistencies, such as ambiguous object positions from different camera views or conflicting local and global motions.
results: I2VControl achieved superior precision in object dragging, with an ObjMC of 17.07 on the Manual Dragging dataset compared to 31.24 for DragAnything, and improved camera control with a RotErr of 0.52 and TransErr of 9.93 on RealEstate10K, outperforming specialized methods.

The framework demonstrated significantly higher motion brush effectiveness, achieving an MSC of 64.59 and IoU of 60.34% on the Object Brush dataset, compared to 6.87 MSC and 15.43% IoU for Motion-I2V.

The system successfully integrates diverse control types, allowing for complex combinations of camera movements, object dragging, and motion brush effects within a single video, and its adapter proved effective across various base models like Seedance and Wanx 2.1.
solution: A unified framework, I2VControl, was developed to simultaneously manage and integrate camera movement, object dragging, and motion brush functionalities without logical conflicts.
Diverse control signals are reformulated into a consistent dense point trajectory representation, which includes trajectory maps, motion strength maps, and spatial partition/category maps.
A spatial partitioning strategy categorizes the 3D scene into 'motion units' (brush-units, drag-units, borderland), allowing different control types to be orchestrated in specific spatial regions.
An adapter network structure, designed as a plug-in for pre-trained diffusion models, processes the combined control signals, making the system agnostic to specific base model architectures.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at ByteDance developed I2VControl, a unified framework that integrates camera movement, object dragging, and motion brush controls for video synthesis from a single image. The system demonstrates improved precision and visual fidelity across various tasks, achieving lower ObjMC for dragging and lower RotErr/TransErr for camera control compared to specialized methods, while enabling complex, conflict-free motion combinations.

title: I2VControl: Disentangled and Unified Video Motion Synthesis Control

abstract: Generating videos guided by camera trajectories poses significant challenges in achieving consistency and generalizability, particularly when both camera and object motions are present. Existing approaches often attempt to learn these motions separately, which may lead to confusion regarding the relative motion between the camera and the objects. To address this challenge, we propose a novel approach that integrates both camera and object motions by converting them into the motion of corresponding pixels. Utilizing a stable diffusion network, we effectively learn reference motion maps in relation to the specified camera trajectory. These maps, along with an extracted semantic object prior, are then fed into an image-to-video network to generate the desired video that can accurately follow the designated camera trajectory while maintaining consistent object motions. Extensive experiments verify that our model outperforms SOTA methods by a large margin.
title: MotionFlow:Learning Implicit Motion Flow for Complex Camera Trajectory Control in Video Generation

abstract: Human-Object Interaction (HOI) video reenactment with realistic motion remains a frontier in expressive digital human creation. Existing approaches primarily handle simple image-plane motion (e.g., in-plane translations), struggling with complex non-planar manipulations like out-of-plane reorientation. In this paper, we propose MVHOI, a two-stage HOI video reenactment framework that bridges multi-view reference conditions and video foundation models via a 3D Foundation Model (3DFM). The 3DFM first produces view-consistent object priors conditioned on implicit motion dynamics across novel viewpoints. A controllable video generation model then synthesizes high-fidelity object texture by incorporating multi-view reference images, ensuring appearance consistency via a reasonable retrieval mechanism. By enabling these two stages to mutually reinforce one another during the inference phase, our framework shows superior performance in generating long-duration HOI videos with intricate object manipulations. Extensive experiments show substantial improvements over prior approaches, especially for HOI with complex 3D object manipulations.
title: MVHOI: Bridge Multi-view Condition to Complex Human-Object Interaction Video Reenactment via 3D Foundation Model

abstract: Generating human videos with realistic and controllable motions is a challenging task. While existing methods can generate visually compelling videos, they lack separate control over four key video elements: foreground subject, background video, human trajectory and action patterns. In this paper, we propose a decomposed human motion control and video generation framework that explicitly decouples motion from appearance, subject from background, and action from trajectory, enabling flexible mix-and-match composition of these elements. Concretely, we first build a ground-aware 3D world coordinate system and perform motion editing directly in the 3D space. Trajectory control is implemented by unprojecting edited 2D trajectories into 3D with focal-length calibration and coordinate transformation, followed by speed alignment and orientation adjustment; actions are supplied by a motion bank or generated via text-to-motion methods. Then, based on modern text-to-video diffusion transformer models, we inject the subject as tokens for full attention, concatenate the background along the channel dimension, and add motion (trajectory and action) control signals by addition. Such a design opens up the possibility for us to generate realistic videos of anyone doing anything anywhere. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets and real-world cases demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on both element-wise controllability and overall video quality.
title: RealisMotion: Decomposed Human Motion Control and Video Generation in the World Space

abstract: With the success of 2D and 3D visual generative models, there is growing interest in generating 4D content. Existing methods primarily rely on text prompts to produce 4D content, but they often fall short of accurately defining complex or rare motions. To address this limitation, we propose MagicPose4D, a novel framework for refined control over both appearance and motion in 4D generation. Unlike current 4D generation methods, MagicPose4D accepts monocular videos or mesh sequences as motion prompts, enabling precise and customizable motion control. MagicPose4D comprises two key modules: (i) Dual-Phase 4D Reconstruction Module, which operates in two phases. The first phase focuses on capturing the model's shape using accurate 2D supervision and less accurate but geometrically informative 3D pseudo-supervision without imposing skeleton constraints. The second phase extracts the 3D motion (skeleton poses) using more accurate pseudo-3D supervision, obtained in the first phase and introduces kinematic chain-based skeleton constraints to ensure physical plausibility. Additionally, we propose a Global-local Chamfer loss that aligns the overall distribution of predicted mesh vertices with the supervision while maintaining part-level alignment without extra annotations. (ii) Cross-category Motion Transfer Module, which leverages the extracted motion from the 4D reconstruction module and uses a kinematic-chain-based skeleton to achieve cross-category motion transfer. It ensures smooth transitions between frames through dynamic rigidity, facilitating robust generalization without additional training. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that MagicPose4D significantly improves the accuracy and consistency of 4D content generation, outperforming existing methods in various benchmarks.
title: MagicPose4D: Crafting Articulated Models with Appearance and Motion Control

abstract: Compositional video generation aims to synthesize multiple instances with diverse appearance and motion, which is widely applicable in real-world scenarios. However, current approaches mainly focus on binding semantics, neglecting to understand diverse motion categories specified in prompts. In this paper, we propose a motion factorization framework that decomposes complex motion into three primary categories: motionlessness, rigid motion, and non-rigid motion. Specifically, our framework follows a planning before generation paradigm. (1) During planning, we reason about motion laws on the motion graph to obtain frame-wise changes in the shape and position of each instance. This alleviates semantic ambiguities in the user prompt by organizing it into a structured representation of instances and their interactions. (2) During generation, we modulate the synthesis of distinct motion categories in a disentangled manner. Conditioned on the motion cues, guidance branches stabilize appearance in motionless regions, preserve rigid-body geometry, and regularize local non-rigid deformations. Crucially, our two modules are model-agnostic, which can be seamlessly incorporated into various diffusion model architectures. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves impressive performance in motion synthesis on real-world benchmarks. Our code will be released soon.

keyInsights: Factorizing motion into distinct categories and applying separate guidance for each effectively addresses the problem of limited motion diversity and consistency.
Leveraging LLMs for structured motion reasoning from text prompts helps resolve semantic ambiguities, leading to more coherent and plausible instance movements.

Specialized, disentangled guidance mechanisms can be integrated model-agnostically into existing video diffusion architectures to modulate motion characteristics without requiring extensive retraining.
originalProblem: Existing compositional video generation models often produce limited motion diversity, resulting in generic dynamics for different instances.
Semantic ambiguity in user prompts frequently leads to inconsistent motion paths or abnormal size variations when translated into bounding box sequences.
Current uniform diffusion guidance fails to differentiate between various motion categories, conflating dynamics and producing implausible results.
results: The framework consistently improved all five "Temporal Quality" metrics (Subject Consistency, Background Consistency, Temporal Flickering, Motion Smoothness, Dynamic Degree) on CVGBench-m and CVGBench-p benchmarks.
Integrating the framework with CogVideoX-2B improved Subject Consistency from 91.00% to 98.27% and Background Consistency from 90.85% to 97.73%.
Ablation studies confirmed that both the Structured Motion Reasoning module and the individual disentangled guidance branches were essential for enhancing motion generation quality and diversity.
solution: A training-free framework is introduced with a "planning before generation" paradigm, decomposing complex motion into distinct categories: motionlessness, rigid, and non-rigid.
The Structured Motion Reasoning (SMR) module uses a large language model to convert ambiguous user prompts into a motion graph, inferring structured spatial-temporal layouts for each instance.

The Disentangled Motion Guidance (DMG) module employs specialized guidance branches to stabilize appearance for motionless instances, preserve geometric invariance for rigid bodies, and regularize local deformations for non-rigid movements within video diffusion models.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Sichuan University and collaborators developed a training-free framework for compositional video generation that disentangles motion into distinct categories. This framework enhances motion diversity and consistency by resolving semantic ambiguities and applying specialized guidance for motionless, rigid, and non-rigid movements, yielding improved temporal quality across benchmarks.

title: Training-free Motion Factorization for Compositional Video Generation

abstract: Existing text-driven motion generation methods often treat synthesis as a bidirectional mapping between language and motion, but remain limited in capturing the causal logic of action execution and the human intentions that drive behavior. The absence of visual grounding further restricts precision and personalization, as language alone cannot specify fine-grained spatiotemporal details. We propose MoGIC, a unified framework that integrates intention modeling and visual priors into multimodal motion synthesis. By jointly optimizing multimodal-conditioned motion generation and intention prediction, MoGIC uncovers latent human goals, leverages visual priors to enhance generation, and exhibits versatile multimodal generative capability. We further introduce a mixture-of-attention mechanism with adaptive scope to enable effective local alignment between conditional tokens and motion subsequences. To support this paradigm, we curate Mo440H, a 440-hour benchmark from 21 high-quality motion datasets. Experiments show that after finetuning, MoGIC reduces FID by 38.6\% on HumanML3D and 34.6\% on Mo440H, surpasses LLM-based methods in motion captioning with a lightweight text head, and further enables intention prediction and vision-conditioned generation, advancing controllable motion synthesis and intention understanding. The code is available at this https URL

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling human intention ("what to do") alongside motion execution ("how to do") significantly improves the realism and contextual appropriateness of generated movements by capturing causal action logic.

Integrating weak visual priors (low-frame-rate images) provides crucial spatiotemporal details and environmental context, overcoming linguistic ambiguity and enabling more precise and personalized motion synthesis.
A dynamic mixture-of-attention mechanism effectively aligns heterogeneous and partially corresponding multimodal inputs (text, vision, motion) at varying granularities.
originalProblem: Existing text-to-motion (T2M) methods often rely on superficial pattern matching, failing to capture the causal logic of action execution or underlying human intentions.
Current motion generation approaches lack visual grounding, leading to ambiguous, imprecise, and less personalized motion synthesis from language alone.
Limitations in data availability, quality (e.g., reliance on pose estimation from web videos), and content bias hinder the development of robust multimodal motion models.
results: MoGIC reduced FID on HumanML3D by 38.6% (to 2.999) and on the new Mo440H dataset by 34.6% for Language-to-Motion (L2M) generation, demonstrating superior quality and alignment.

The Intention Prediction Head (IPH) showed competitive motion captioning performance, outperforming several LLM-based baselines in metrics like BLEU and ROUGE, indicating strong semantic interpretation.

Integration of visual modality enabled biomechanically realistic motions in tasks like weightlifting and consistently improved performance across V2M, LV2M, and vision-based in-between tasks by providing complementary spatiotemporal hints.
solution: A unified framework incorporates modality-specific encoders for motion, language (frozen CLIP), and vision (low-frame-rate frames) to process diverse inputs.
A Conditional Masked Transformer (CMT) with semantic-level modulation and an adaptive Mixture-of-Attention mechanism aligns multimodal context with motion tokens.

Disentangled generation heads, an Intention Prediction Head (T5-style decoder) and a Motion Generation Head (continuous-time interpolant model), separately handle linguistic intention and continuous motion.
paper_summary.summary: MOGIC, developed by researchers at The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou), introduces a unified framework for generating human motion by explicitly modeling human intention and integrating visual context from low-frame-rate image sequences. The approach significantly improved FID by 38.6% on the HumanML3D benchmark and reduced it by 34.6% on the new Mo440H dataset, enhancing precision and control in motion synthesis.

title: MoGIC: Boosting Motion Generation via Intention Understanding and Visual Context

abstract: Pose-guided video generation refers to controlling the motion of subjects in generated video through a sequence of poses. It enables precise control over subject motion and has important applications in animation. However, current pose-guided video generation methods are limited to accepting only human poses as input, thus generalizing poorly to pose of other subjects. To address this issue, we propose PoseAnything, the first universal pose-guided video generation framework capable of handling both human and non-human characters, supporting arbitrary skeletal inputs. To enhance consistency preservation during motion, we introduce Part-aware Temporal Coherence Module, which divides the subject into different parts, establishes part correspondences, and computes cross-attention between corresponding parts across frames to achieve fine-grained part-level consistency. Additionally, we propose Subject and Camera Motion Decoupled CFG, a novel guidance strategy that, for the first time, enables independent camera movement control in pose-guided video generation, by separately injecting subject and camera motion control information into the positive and negative anchors of CFG. Furthermore, we present XPose, a high-quality public dataset containing 50,000 non-human pose-video pairs, along with an automated pipeline for annotation and filtering. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Pose-Anything significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both effectiveness and generalization.

keyInsights: Universal pose guidance for arbitrary skeletal inputs can be achieved by carefully integrating pose latent representations via channel-wise concatenation within a diffusion model.
Fine-grained appearance consistency and temporal coherence are substantially improved by explicitly modeling and matching subject parts across frames using a dedicated cross-attention mechanism.
Independent control over both subject motion and camera movement can be achieved by leveraging Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) to inject opposing camera control signals into negative anchors.
originalProblem: Existing pose-guided video generation methods were primarily human-centric, limiting their application to a diverse range of non-human characters.
Prior approaches struggled to maintain consistent appearance and temporal coherence of subjects, particularly during complex movements or large pose changes.
Conventional pose-guided methods lacked explicit control over camera movements, limiting the expressive power and cinematic quality of generated videos.
results: PoseAnything achieved state-of-the-art performance on the TikTok human dataset, with the best PSNR (31.50), SSIM (0.836), LPIPS (0.224), and FVD (133.95) compared to existing methods.
On the new XPose non-human benchmark, PoseAnything outperformed drag-and-control baselines across all metrics (e.g., FVD of 99.97), demonstrating superior non-human pose control and realism.
The Subject and Camera Motion Decoupled CFG successfully enables precise, independent control over both character actions and camera movements simultaneously, preventing mutual interference.
solution: PoseAnything proposes a unified framework capable of accommodating both human and non-human arbitrary skeletal inputs to control subject motion in generated videos.
It introduces a Part-aware Temporal Coherence Module (PTCM) that ensures fine-grained, part-level consistency by segmenting subjects and applying cross-attention between matched parts across frames.
A novel Subject and Camera Motion Decoupled CFG strategy allows for independent and controllable camera movements, effectively decoupling subject and camera control signals.
paper_summary.summary: Shanghai Jiao Tong University researchers developed PoseAnything, the first universal framework for pose-guided video generation that handles arbitrary skeletal inputs for both human and non-human characters. It achieves superior appearance consistency and introduces independent camera control, outperforming prior methods on established benchmarks and a newly released non-human dataset.

title: PoseAnything: Universal Pose-guided Video Generation with Part-aware Temporal Coherence

abstract: Controlling both camera motion and object dynamics is essential for coherent and expressive video generation, yet current methods typically handle only one motion type or rely on ambiguous 2D cues that entangle camera-induced parallax with true object movement. We present SymphoMotion, a unified motion-control framework that jointly governs camera trajectories and object dynamics within a single model. SymphoMotion features a Camera Trajectory Control mechanism that integrates explicit camera paths with geometry-aware cues to ensure stable, structurally consistent viewpoint transitions, and an Object Dynamics Control mechanism that combines 2D visual guidance with 3D trajectory embeddings to enable depth-aware, spatially coherent object manipulation. To support large-scale training and evaluation, we further construct RealCOD-25K, a comprehensive real-world dataset containing paired camera poses and object-level 3D trajectories across diverse indoor and outdoor scenes, addressing a key data gap in unified motion control. Extensive experiments and user studies show that SymphoMotion significantly outperforms existing methods in visual fidelity, camera controllability, and object-motion accuracy, establishing a new benchmark for unified motion control in video this http URL and data are publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A unified framework that concurrently controls camera trajectories and object dynamics, rather than isolated control, is crucial for generating more coherent and expressive videos.

Integrating 3D geometric priors for camera control and 3D trajectory embeddings for objects helps disentangle camera-induced parallax from true object motion, enabling more accurate and stable dynamics.

The availability of a comprehensive, real-world dataset with synchronized 3D annotations for both camera and object motion is fundamental for training robust models capable of complex, unified motion control.
originalProblem: Existing video generation methods typically control camera motion or object dynamics independently, leading to unsynchronized behavior and a lack of narrative coherence in dynamic scenes.

Methods relying on 2D cues for object motion control ambiguously entangle camera-induced parallax with true object movement, making them unreliable under camera movement or significant viewpoint changes.

There is a critical lack of large-scale, real-world datasets providing synchronized annotations for both camera poses and object-level 3D trajectories, which hinders the development of unified motion control models.
results: SymphoMotion achieved the lowest Fréchet Image Distance (FID) of 70.47 and Fréchet Video Distance (FVD) of 332.50, demonstrating superior visual fidelity and temporal coherence compared to state-of-the-art methods.
The framework exhibited significantly more accurate object trajectory adherence, achieving a Box-IoU of 61.88, substantially outperforming MotionCtrl (31.42).

It also showed improved camera motion control with the lowest CamTransErr (0.37) and CamRotErr (0.05), alongside high text alignment (CLIPSIM 0.31) and positive ratings in user studies across all metrics.
solution: SymphoMotion, a diffusion-based framework, introduces a Camera Trajectory Control (CTC) mechanism that integrates explicit camera paths with 3D geometric priors to ensure stable and structurally consistent viewpoint transitions.

It implements an Object Dynamics Control (ODC) mechanism that combines 2D visual guidance (bounding boxes derived from 3D projections) with 3D trajectory embeddings for depth-aware, spatially coherent object manipulation.

The authors created RealCOD-25K, a large-scale real-world dataset of 25K video clips with paired camera poses and object-level 3D trajectories, to facilitate training and evaluation of unified motion control models.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, Shanghai Jiaotong University, Nanjing University, and Beihang University developed SymphoMotion, a diffusion-based framework for jointly controlling camera motion and object dynamics in video generation. The model achieves superior visual fidelity, temporal coherence, and motion accuracy through 3D geometric priors and real-world 3D object trajectories, supported by the newly introduced RealCOD-25K dataset.

title: SymphoMotion: Joint Control of Camera Motion and Object Dynamics for Coherent Video Generation

abstract: Recent progress in video generation has led to substantial improvements in visual fidelity, yet ensuring physically consistent motion remains a fundamental challenge. Intuitively, this limitation can be attributed to the fact that real-world object motion unfolds in three-dimensional space, while video observations provide only partial, view-dependent projections of such dynamics. To address these issues, we propose PhysVideo, a two-stage framework that first generates physics-aware orthogonal foreground videos and then synthesizes full videos with background. In the first stage, Phys4View leverages physics-aware attention to capture the influence of physical attributes on motion dynamics, and enhances spatio-temporal consistency by incorporating geometry-enhanced cross-view attention and temporal attention. In the second stage, VideoSyn uses the generated foreground videos as guidance and learns the interactions between foreground dynamics and background context for controllable video synthesis. To support training, we construct PhysMV, a dataset containing 40K scenes, each consisting of four orthogonal viewpoints, resulting in a total of 160K video sequences. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PhysVideo significantly improves physical realism and spatial-temporal coherence over existing video generation methods. Home page: this https URL.

keyInsights: Decoupling the generation of physics-conditioned multi-view foreground motion from background-aware video synthesis effectively enhances overall realism and spatiotemporal consistency.

Integrating physics-aware attention and geometry-enhanced cross-view attention, guided by predicted depth, provides inductive biases that enable data-driven models to learn and adhere to physical laws without explicit 3D simulation during inference.

The use of multi-view data and 3D Gaussian Splatting for dataset creation and as an intermediate structural prior offers crucial supervision for learning 3D geometric consistency within a generative framework.
originalProblem: Current data-driven video generation models often fail to produce physically plausible and spatially consistent motion, frequently violating fundamental physical principles.
Existing methods struggle to incorporate 3D geometric and physical principles into pixel-space video generation, leading to inconsistencies across different viewpoints and over time.

Physics-engine-based simulations provide valid object trajectories but are complex, require explicit 3D representations, and are difficult to integrate into efficient, end-to-end generative frameworks.
results: PhysVideo achieved superior physical realism (GPT-4o Physical Realism score of 0.627) and visual quality (WorldScore Photo 94.09, Motion 93.62) compared to state-of-the-art physics-engine-based and learning-based video generation methods.

The framework consistently generated videos with physically plausible and coherent object motion, maintaining object integrity and demonstrating realistic interactions with foreground and background elements.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of physics-aware attention, geometry-enhanced cross-view attention, and temporal attention modules to improving physical realism and motion consistency within the Phys4View stage.
solution: A two-stage framework, PhysVideo, is proposed, which first generates physics-aware orthogonal multi-view foreground videos (Phys4View) and then synthesizes full videos with backgrounds (VideoSyn).

Phys4View integrates physics-aware attention, conditioned on physical attributes (motion cues, material properties), and geometry-enhanced cross-view attention, utilizing predicted depth maps to enforce multi-view and temporal consistency.

A large-scale, physics-oriented multi-view video dataset, PhysMV, was constructed using 3D Gaussian Splatting and the TaiChi physics engine, providing 40,000 distinct motion sequences from four orthogonal viewpoints for training.
paper_summary.summary: PhysVideo, developed by researchers from Chinese Academy of Sciences and other institutions, introduces a two-stage framework for generating videos with physically plausible and geometrically consistent motion. The method achieves superior physical realism and visual quality, obtaining the highest GPT-4o physical realism score of 0.627, by guiding full video synthesis with physics-aware, multi-view foreground motion, outperforming existing data-driven and physics-engine-based approaches.

title: PhysVideo: Physically Plausible Video Generation with Cross-View Geometry Guidance

abstract: The essence of a video lies in its dynamic motions, including character actions, object movements, and camera movements. While text-to-video generative diffusion models have recently advanced in creating diverse contents, controlling specific motions through text prompts remains a significant challenge. A primary issue is the coupling of appearance and motion, often leading to overfitting on appearance. To tackle this challenge, we introduce MotionCrafter, a novel one-shot instance-guided motion customization method. MotionCrafter employs a parallel spatial-temporal architecture that injects the reference motion into the temporal component of the base model, while the spatial module is independently adjusted for character or style control. To enhance the disentanglement of motion and appearance, we propose an innovative dual-branch motion disentanglement approach, comprising a motion disentanglement loss and an appearance prior enhancement strategy. During training, a frozen base model provides appearance normalization, effectively separating appearance from motion and thereby preserving diversity. Comprehensive quantitative and qualitative experiments, along with user preference tests, demonstrate that MotionCrafter can successfully integrate dynamic motions while preserving the coherence and quality of the base model with a wide range of appearance generation capabilities. Project page: this https URL. Codes are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Leveraging the inherent separation of spatial and temporal modules in 3D U-Nets provides a foundational architectural pathway for disentangling appearance and motion.
A frozen, pre-trained U-Net branch combined with diverse text prompts effectively acts as an 'appearance prior' to prevent appearance overfitting during motion transfer.
A tailored disentanglement loss, using KL divergence to match appearance distributions, successfully preserves motion fidelity while enabling diverse new appearances.
originalProblem: Text prompts alone are often insufficient for specifying intricate or precise dynamic motions in text-to-video generation.
Existing video customization methods frequently couple appearance and motion, leading to overfitting of the reference video's appearance and limiting content diversity.
Many control mechanisms for video generation focus on structural elements rather than specific dynamic actions, or require extensive retraining.
results: Achieves state-of-the-art quantitative performance across diversity, temporal consistency, and motion fidelity metrics, outperforming seven baseline methods.

Qualitatively demonstrates robust motion transfer from reference videos to entirely different subjects or objects specified by text, while maintaining high visual quality and strong appearance disentanglement.
Received the highest user preference scores in a user study across all categories: motion fidelity, appearance diversity, video consistency, and visual quality.
solution: Employs a parallel spatial-temporal architecture that fine-tunes spatial attention modules for appearance and temporal attention modules for motion using a single reference video.
Introduces a dual-branch motion disentanglement scheme, utilizing a frozen U-Net branch as an appearance prior to guide diverse content generation.

Applies a motion disentanglement loss that combines a temporal loss for motion fidelity with an appearance loss (KL divergence) to align the trainable branch's appearance distribution with the diverse prior.
paper_summary.summary: MotionCrafter introduces a one-shot method for customizing dynamic motions in text-to-video diffusion models, enabling users to transfer motion from a single reference video to new content described by text. The approach disentangles motion from appearance, preventing overfitting and achieving state-of-the-art performance in motion fidelity and appearance diversity.

title: MotionCrafter: One-Shot Motion Customization of Diffusion Models

abstract: Motion-based controllable video generation offers the potential for creating captivating visual content. Existing methods typically necessitate model training to encode particular motion cues or incorporate fine-tuning to inject certain motion patterns, resulting in limited flexibility and generalization. In this work, we propose MotionClone, a training-free framework that enables motion cloning from reference videos to versatile motion-controlled video generation, including text-to-video and image-to-video. Based on the observation that the dominant components in temporal-attention maps drive motion synthesis, while the rest mainly capture noisy or very subtle motions, MotionClone utilizes sparse temporal attention weights as motion representations for motion guidance, facilitating diverse motion transfer across varying scenarios. Meanwhile, MotionClone allows for the direct extraction of motion representation through a single denoising step, bypassing the cumbersome inversion processes and thus promoting both efficiency and flexibility. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MotionClone exhibits proficiency in both global camera motion and local object motion, with notable superiority in terms of motion fidelity, textual alignment, and temporal consistency.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Temporal attention maps within video generation models inherently encode rich motion information that can be leveraged directly for motion cloning.
Focusing on sparse, dominant components of temporal attention maps is crucial for effective motion synthesis and disentangling motion from structure.
Motion guidance is most effective when applied strategically in early denoising steps and specific temporal attention layers, ensuring strong motion alignment without degrading visual quality.
originalProblem: Existing controllable video generation methods often require extensive training or fine-tuning, limiting flexibility and generalization.
Dense motion cue methods entangle motion with structural elements, restricting transferability and semantic alignment with new prompts.
Trajectory-based methods capture only coarse movements, failing to delineate finer, localized motions.
results: Outperformed all baselines in user studies across motion preservation (3.69), appearance diversity (4.31), textual alignment (4.15), and temporal consistency (4.28).
Qualitatively demonstrated superior performance in cloning complex camera and localized object motions while maintaining strong textual alignment and appearance diversity.
Exhibits versatility, successfully integrating with both text-to-video and image-to-video generation tasks through efficient single-step motion representation extraction.
solution: A training-free framework leveraging sparse temporal attention weights as motion representations from pre-trained video diffusion models.
Extracts motion representations from reference videos in a single noise-adding and denoising step, enhancing efficiency.
Applies "primary motion control" by selectively constraining only the dominant components of temporal attention maps early in the denoising process.
paper_summary.summary: MOTIONCLONE is a training-free framework enabling motion cloning from reference videos for controllable text-to-video and image-to-video generation. It leverages sparse temporal attention weights as motion representations, demonstrating superior motion preservation, appearance diversity, and textual alignment compared to state-of-the-art baselines in user studies.

title: MotionClone: Training-Free Motion Cloning for Controllable Video Generation

abstract: Real-world videos naturally portray complex interactions among distinct physical objects, effectively forming dynamic compositions of visual elements. However, most current video generation models synthesize scenes holistically and therefore lack mechanisms for explicit compositional manipulation. To address this limitation, we propose HECTOR, a generative pipeline that enables fine-grained compositional control. In contrast to prior methods,HECTOR supports hybrid reference conditioning, allowing generation to be simultaneously guided by static images and/or dynamic videos. Moreover, users can explicitly specify the trajectory of each referenced element, precisely controlling its location, scale, and speed (see Figure1). This design allows the model to synthesize coherent videos that satisfy complex spatiotemporal constraints while preserving high-fidelity adherence to references. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HECTOR achieves superior visual quality, stronger reference preservation, and improved motion controllability compared with existing approaches.
title: HECTOR: Hybrid Editable Compositional Object References for Video Generation

abstract:
In recent years, large-scale pre-trained diffusion transformer models have
made significant progress in video generation. While current DiT models can
produce high-definition, high-frame-rate, and highly diverse videos, there is a
lack of fine-grained control over the video content. Controlling the motion of
subjects in videos using only prompts is challenging, especially when it comes
